A standard 2.5–3x open works well here: it builds the pot when I hit an Ace, prices out speculative callers, and defines my range. I’ll raise to 300 to open my range and avoid playing a multiway pot OOP. Plan postflop: C-bet small on dry boards, proceed cautiously vs big resistance or 3-bets (fold most A6o hands to large 3-bets from the button; consider calling small 3-bets depending on odds and position).

I am on the button with J4o, a speculative offsuit hand with large gaps and low playability. Minni, an aggressive UTG raiser from hand history, opened to 300 into a 450 pot. This hand has poor equity against a typical UTG range, dominated post-flop potential, and doesn't warrant calling from deep stacks. Folding preserves my stack for better spots; no need to speculate out of position against aggression.

I am in the Small Blind with K-7 offsuit. Minni opened for 3x from UTG. While K-7o has some raw equity, it is a weak hand to play out of position against an UTG open. Calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop without the lead, and a 3-bet would be a bluff against a range that Minni has shown to be relatively tight-aggressive in early positions. Preserving my stack for better opportunities is the standard play here.

Q6o (Queen-six offsuit) is a weak hand that doesn't warrant calling from the big blind against a UTG raise. Minni's raise from early position indicates a reasonable holding, and my hand will struggle significantly if called - I'm likely dominated if Minni holds any Queen with a better kicker, and I have poor equity against most of Minni's opening range. With a relatively short stack (5450) and early in the tournament (hand 39 of 150), I should preserve chips for stronger spots rather than invest in marginal situations. The pot odds (2.25:1) don't provide sufficient compensation given my hand's weakness and position disadvantage post-flop.

4-handed play, UTG position with A♣2♣. Suited Ace with nut flush potential and strong blocker. Playable hand in short-handed game with good post-flop playability. Standard open-raise to 3x BB (300) to build pot or steal blinds. Deep stacks allow for implied odds if we hit. Opponents show aggression but this hand warrants raising over limping or folding.

I am on the button with Q9o. Elon has raised to 3x the big blind from UTG. Q9o is too weak to call a raise from early position, as it is likely dominated by Elon's opening range (which typically includes high pairs and strong broadway cards). A 3-bet bluff is possible but not ideal with this hand. Given the stack sizes and the strength of the opener, the most prudent play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

Calling with 8-J from small blind vs UTG raise. At 1.8:1 pot odds with a hand that has straight draw potential and decent kicker, this is a reasonable call. Stack depth is healthy and there's one more player to act who may fold or call, allowing me to play this connector in position relative to the stacks.

Facing an UTG raise to 300 with the small blind cold-calling creates a multiway pot. My hand Q♠4♣ is a weak offsuit queen with poor kicker and very limited post-flop playability — it will be dominated by a typical UTG raising range (AQ, KQ, Qx, JJ+, etc.) and has negligible fold equity if I try to 3-bet. Calling 200 into a 700 pot to see a flop would put me in tough spots out of position against two players with stronger ranges. 3-betting to the minimum (500) is also unattractive — it bloats the pot with a poor hand and invites 4-bets or calls from hands that dominate me. With healthy stack depth and many better spots to pick, the +EV play is to fold and conserve chips for a stronger opportunity.

Betting top pair with a strong kicker on a relatively dry flop. The 300 chip bet is aggressive enough to take control from the UTG aggressor, extracts value from his weaker holdings (AQ, KQ, pair with worse kicker), and represents proper pot control. My J8 is well-positioned against his likely range, and this sizing leaves me healthy stack management with 4850 remaining.

Preflop, I raised Ac2c suited from UTG in a 4-handed game, which is standard for its playability and flush potential. Flop 5c Js 2d gives me bottom pair (2s) with Ace kicker, which has decent showdown value and blocks strong hands like A2 or 22. I have backdoor flush draw and some straight potential. Heads-up against Claude, who called my raise preflop and now bets 300 into a 1000 pot (3x pot bet sizing? Wait, small bet). This bet could be a stab at a dry board or weak Jx. My range is perceived as stronger as preflop raiser. Folding is too tight OOP with pair + kicker. Raising risks overplaying vs possible Jx/set and commits more OOP. Calling 300 keeps the pot manageable (pot becomes 1600), realizes equity, controls pot size, and allows me to see turn with position advantage post-flop (acting last). With deep stacks (14k effective), implied odds if improves, and history shows Claude checks/folds often. Long-term EV positive to continue with this holding.

Two pair (Jacks and Eights) on the turn is a very strong holding against Elon's likely UTG range. The 8h doesn't improve most of his probable holdings (AK, AQ, KQ, lower pairs), making this an ideal spot to extract value with a mid-size bet. A 550-chip bet maintains good pot control, gets paid by weaker hands, and leaves me 4300 in chips for the river action. This sizing is standard value betting (roughly 42% of the pot) and balances extracting chips with stack preservation.

River decision with QK on a 2d-Th-4c-As-Jc board against a passive opponent.

**Hand Analysis:**
My actual holding: Qh-Kd creates an ace-high straight (T-J-Q-K-A), which is a very strong hand on this runout.

**Opponent Pattern:**
Flash has now checked on flop, turn, and river - three consecutive streets. This is an extremely weak action pattern indicating:
- He missed the board or his hand is marginal at best
- Unlikely to have a strong hand given he didn't bet once
- Typical patterns suggest weak ace (A9, A8), mid-pair (77-99), or complete air

**Position & Stack Dynamics:**
- Pot: 4400
- My stack: 3950 (small but adequate)
- Flash stack: 4550
- I'm last to act with initiative

**Decision Logic:**
With an ace-high straight, I have a premium holding that crushes Flash's likely range given his complete passivity. A river value bet is clearly indicated. Flash's three-street checking pattern means he won't call large amounts, but the hand strength justifies extracting value regardless.

A bet of 1800 (~40% of pot) is appropriate because it:
1. Values my strong hand appropriately without overcommitting
2. Gives Flash a reasonable fold-able amount (he can fold 1800 but might call with a pair)
3. Extracts value if Flash calls with anything weaker
4. Leaves me 2150 chips if he folds, or commits most of my remaining stack if he shoves
5. Is standard river value bet sizing

The math favors betting: Flash's passive line screams weakness, and my straight crushes his most likely holdings.

The hand is strong enough for UTG, and raising gives better control than calling. With three opponents remaining, showing strength early helps build the pot with a playable hand. The raise also sets up good position post-flop.

Claude opened UTG to 300 (pot 450). On the button with K♠4♦ I’m facing a likely strong, UTG opening range and two players still to act (SB Elon and short-stacked BB Flash ~21BB) who can call or shove. K4o is frequently dominated and has poor post‑flop playability; calling 300 is a marginal, low‑equity line and a small 3‑bet would invite jams or tough decisions. With a healthy stack I’ll preserve chips for better spots rather than defend with a weak, dominated hand — fold.

Qh2c is a very weak offsuit hand with minimal high card value and no connectivity or suited potential. Facing an UTG raise from Claude, who has shown aggression in prior hands, his range is strong (likely premium pairs, big aces, broadway cards). I'm out of position in the SB, making post-flop play difficult even if I hit something. Pot odds to call 250 into 450 are about 2:1, but equity against his range is too low (<30%) to justify. Folding preserves my deep stack (100x BB) for better opportunities in this multi-way tournament spot.

I am in the Big Blind with QJs, a strong suited broadway hand. Claude raised from UTG to 300. With 2,100 chips (21 BB), I am relatively short-stacked, but QJs is too strong to fold and has great playability. I am getting excellent pot odds (200 to call into a pot of 450). While a shove is an option to maximize fold equity, calling allows me to see a flop and realize my equity while maintaining some maneuverability, even though I'll be out of position. The low stack-to-pot ratio (SPR) post-flop means I'll likely be committed if I hit any significant part of the board.

I have completely missed this flop with QJs. The board is Ad-As-7d, and since Claude raised from UTG, his range is heavily weighted towards strong Aces (AK, AQ, AJ, AT) and pocket pairs. While I have two overcards to the 7, any Ace in Claude's hand makes me drawing nearly dead. Even if he has a pocket pair like 88-KK, I am behind. There is no reason to lead out here into the pre-flop aggressor on such a static board. My plan is to check and see how he responds; I will likely fold to any significant bet as my stack is too shallow to float or bluff on this texture.

Betting 400 with a full house (Aces full of Sevens). Flash checked, indicating weakness. With a monster hand, I need to build value while keeping the short-stacked opponent engaged. A bet of approximately 2/3 pot is standard value bet sizing on the flop that should induce calls from weaker hands without being so large that it pushes Flash out of the hand.

I have completely missed this flop with QsJs. The board is Ad-As-7d, which heavily favors Claude's UTG opening range containing many strong Aces (AK, AQ, AJ, AT). Claude has followed up with a continuation bet of 400 into a 650 pot. With my stack at 1,900 chips (19 BB), I cannot afford to float out of position with zero equity or draws. Even if Claude is bluffing with a hand like KQ, I have no way to profitably continue given the board texture and my lack of a pair or significant draw. Folding is the only disciplined play here to preserve my remaining stack.

UTG with Th5d is well outside a standard open-raise range. T5o has poor post‑flop playability, is frequently dominated, and will be tough to play multiway or vs. 3-bets — no reason to create action from early position. With a deep 175bb stack I’ll preserve chips and wait for a much stronger hand or a better position. Fold.

Preflop on the button with AJo, a strong hand in position against the blinds. Minni folded UTG, pot is 150 (blinds), to call 100. Standard play is to raise for value and position. Flash in SB is short-stacked at 1850 (~18BB), may shove wide but AJo plays well postflop and has good equity vs shoves. Claude deep in BB. Raising to 300 (3x BB) is standard sizing to build pot, isolate, and respect minraise. Avoid limping to maintain initiative.

I am currently the short stack with 18.5 BB. Elon has opened from the button for 3x, which is a wide range, but Ace-Deuce offsuit (A2o) performs poorly out of position and is easily dominated by his better Aces or pairs. Calling 250 chips (nearly 15% of my stack) to see a flop out of position with a marginal hand is suboptimal. While a shove is a possible high-variance play, with 18.5 BB I still have enough of a cushion to wait for a better hand or a more favorable situation to commit my tournament life.

K4 offsuit is too weak to play out of position in the big blind. Despite getting 2.25:1 pot odds, the hand lacks sufficient equity and faces significant positional disadvantage post-flop. The weak 4 kicker means I'm often dominated when the King hits. With a healthy 10,350 stack and no desperation, this is a straightforward fold to maintain disciplined play.

72 offsuit is one of the worst possible starting hands in poker, especially from early position (UTG) where I have to act first pre-flop. Folding preserves my stack for better spots. No position advantage, low equity against any calling range, and multiway pots would be disastrous. Opponents have shown aggression in raises, so entering with trash risks spew. Tight play maximizes long-term EV over 150 hands.

I am on the button with AcJc, a premium starting hand in a 4-handed game. My stack is relatively short at 18.5 big blinds compared to the other players. With Elon folding, I am first to act. Raising here is the standard play to take control of the pot and potentially take down the blinds or get value from weaker hands that might call from the blinds. A standard 3x raise is appropriate for my stack size.

T3o is one of the weakest hands in poker. While Flash is short-stacked at 1,550 and may be raising with a wide range from the button, I have minimal equity against his likely holdings. Being out of position in the small blind with this hand, combined with the possibility of Minni defending with a stronger range in the big blind, makes this a clear fold. The 250 chips to call isn't worth the long-term value leak of playing T3o out of position with poor equity. I should focus on preserving my stack and playing premium hands in better situations.

Facing a BTN open to 300; I’m in the big blind with 7♦6♣. Flash is very short (≈15.5bb), so his open range is wide but he can also shove. 7-6o is a speculative, but playable, hand — not a 3-bet or shove candidate given his stack and my deep stack. Calling 200 more (300 total committed including my BB) to see a flop is the best option: the pot odds and the villain’s wide range justify a flat-call to realize equity and play postflop. Plan: see the flop and proceed cautiously — fold to significant shove/aggression unless I’ve improved (pair or strong draw).

I’m in the BB with 7♦6♣ on Qc Qd 9d. I have no pair, only a backdoor diamond and very little equity versus a BTN open — Flash (≈15.5bb) opened wide preflop but his range on this paired board contains many Qx, 9x and pocket pairs/overpairs. Betting as a bluff is unattractive: if I bet and he shoves I’d face a shove call that requires ~41% equity to justify and I don’t have it. Best line is pot control — check and see what he does. If he checks back I get to a cheap showdown; if he fires a small c‑bet I can consider a call for the backdoor odds, but I will fold to any meaningful shove or large bet since my hand has negligible showdown equity.

On this paired board of QcQd9d, my Ace-high has decent showdown value but is vulnerable to any Nine or pocket pair. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and am now in position, checking back is a strong play. It allows me to realize my equity and keep the pot small, which is important given my relatively short stack of 15.5 big blinds. Betting risks a check-raise that would force me to fold my equity. By checking, I can also potentially induce a bluff on the turn from weaker hands or see a free card to improve to top pair or a backdoor draw.

Heads‑up vs the short BTN (≈15.5bb). I hold 7♦6♣ on Q♣ Q♦ 9♦ T♦ — I currently have no pair but I did pick up a big drawing package: 3 diamonds on board + my 7♦ = a 4‑card diamond (9 diamond outs) plus three non‑diamond 8s that give me a 6‑7‑8‑9‑10 straight. That’s 12 unique outs on the river (~12/46 ≈ 26%).

Even with ~26% to improve, I’m still behind any Qx/9x/Tx or pocket pair right now. Flash is very short and can (and often will) shove if I show aggression; a shove would price me for ~70% equity to call (I can’t), so I should avoid getting raised off a bet. Betting as a semi‑bluff could win the pot sometimes, but it also invites an all‑in I must fold to or commit with poor equity.

Best line is pot control: check and take a free card to realize my ~26% equity. If Flash leads small on the river I can call or raise depending on sizing and read; I will fold to a turn shove. Action: check.

On the turn, the board has become extremely coordinated with three diamonds and a pair of Queens (Qc Qd 9d Td). While I have a gutshot straight draw with my Jack, I do not have a diamond, which is a major concern on this board. Minni has checked for the second time, suggesting she might be weak or perhaps pot-controlling with a marginal hand or a slow-played monster. However, betting into this board without a diamond or a made hand is risky, as I could easily be check-raised or called by any diamond or a Queen. Checking back allows me to see the river for free, realize my equity, and potentially catch a King to make a straight or simply reach showdown with Ace-high if the river is safe. Given my stack size, preserving chips is a priority over a low-probability semi-bluff.

Board is Qc Qd 9d Td 5h and I hold 7♦6♣. I missed both my diamond and straight outs on the river — I have essentially no showdown value. Flash (BTN) checked flop and turn, showing weakness or pot control; that makes a river bluff thin because many hands that beat me (Qx, 9x, Tx, pocket pairs) will not fold to a small river stab, and a bet invites a shove from a short stack that I cannot profitably call. With negligible equity and good reason to avoid getting raised/shoved, I take the pot control line: check and fold to any river bet. If Flash checks back I get to a free showdown (no cost), which is preferable to risking chips on a low-fold-equity bluff.

The board is highly coordinated with three diamonds and a pair of Queens. My Ace-high (Ac Jc) has some showdown value against missed draws or total air. Minni has checked for the third street in a row, which often indicates weakness or a marginal made hand that is content to reach showdown. Since my hand is unlikely to fold out better hands (like a 9, a 10, or a small flush) and I have some equity to win at showdown, checking back is the most sensible play to preserve my remaining stack of 12.5 big blinds.

In a 4-max game, being UTG is equivalent to being in the Cutoff. While an Ace is often worth a raise in short-handed play, Ace-Deuce offsuit is at the very bottom of my opening range. Given my current stack of 22 BBs (2200 chips) and the fact that the other three players have significantly larger stacks and have shown a willingness to contest pots, I prefer to fold this hand and wait for a stronger holding or a better situational opportunity to commit chips.

Calling with 9-8 offsuit on the button. Strong position advantage against two blind players, reasonable pot odds (150 to 100), and the hand has straight potential. The cost is minimal relative to my 10,300 stack, and I can play profitably post-flop with positional advantage.

Holding A♠T♣ in the SB facing a single limp from the button. Pot is 250 and I only need to put in 50 to complete, but ATo is a hand that performs poorly multiway and is worth taking the initiative with. I have a big stack (≈172bb) so I can apply pressure and isolate the limper. A min-raise to 200 is too small and invites action; I’ll raise to 300 total (3x BB) to fold out weaker hands and play a cleaner pot heads-up or as the aggressor if called. Postflop plan: c-bet on A/T-high or dry boards, proceed cautiously on connected/flushy boards, and fold to a large, non-pot-committing 3‑bet since I’m OOP with a marginal hand.

K3o is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability postflop, dominated by any king in opponents' ranges. Facing a standard raise to 300 from Minni in the SB after a limp from Claude on the button, the pot odds of calling 200 into 500 (~3.5:1) are not sufficient given my equity against Minni's likely strong raising range (which includes many better kings and pairs). Folding preserves my stack for better spots in this tournament phase.

I'm holding 9-8 offsuit on the button with good pot odds (2.5:1, needing to call 200 into 500). Minni raised from the small blind to 300 after I called 100. Given my position advantage, the hand's straight potential, and favorable equity math (9-8o has ~42% equity against a typical SB raising range, well above the 28% break-even point at these odds), calling is the mathematically sound play. My stack is large enough to play profitably post-flop with positional advantage. Raising doesn't make sense here as 9-8o is not a premium hand, and Minni has a large stack showing strength.

I was the preflop aggressor (raised to isolate). On 8♦ 9♣ 9♥ my A♠T♣ missed but I still have initiative and decent fold equity vs Claude’s wide calling range. I also block some A9 combos with the Ace, reducing the most dangerous trip combos slightly. This is a good spot for a continuation bet to pick up the pot or charge weaker broadway hands/pocket pairs; I’ll use a ~½‑pot size to balance fold equity and pot control. If Claude raises big I’ll fold OOP with no pair; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously and re-evaluate on the turn.

I hold 9d-8h on a flop of 8d-9c-9h, which gives me a full house (nines full of eights) - an extremely strong hand. Minni has bet 400 into a 700-chip pot on the flop. With a full house, I'm crushing virtually any hand in Minni's pre-flop raising range (overpairs, AK, etc.). The critical decision is whether to call or raise. Given my hand's strength, a call is optimal here: (1) Keeping Minni in the hand allows me to extract maximum value over subsequent streets rather than risk a fold from a raise; (2) The pot odds are excellent (1500 to 400, or 3.75:1); (3) With position on the button, I can control the action post-turn; (4) My stack (10,000) is sufficient to apply maximum pressure if needed on later streets. Raising now risks scaring Minni away when I want them to continue building the pot. Calling maximizes expected value.

I have A♠T♣ on 8♦‑9♣‑9♥‑5♣ — only ace‑high and no real backdoor. I was the flop aggressor and got called, so Claude’s continuing range is heavily weighted toward hands that already beat me (9x trips, 88/set, TT+/two‑pair, etc.). The 5♣ is a blank and barreling again OOP with essentially zero equity is a high‑risk bluff; I should avoid bloating the pot. I’ll check to control the size of the pot, plan to fold to any sizable bet or raise, and only consider calling a very small, suspicious turn bet. If Claude checks back I get a free river to reevaluate.

I have a full house (nines full of eights) on the turn. Minni checked after betting the flop, suggesting a weaker hand. This is an optimal situation to extract value with a standard bet of 1000 chips into the 1500-chip pot. This builds the pot while keeping Minni interested in the hand, avoiding both missed value (checking) and overcommitting (oversized bet that induces a fold). My position and hand strength allow me to control this hand profitably.

I raised preflop and c-bet the flop but missed the board with A♠T♣. After I checked the turn Claude bets 1,000 into a 2,500 pot. He called the flop, so his continuing range is heavily weighted toward hands that beat me (9x trips/sets, 8x, medium/over pairs, some made two‑pair). Calling 1,000 to potentially win ~4,500 requires ~22% equity — AHigh has far less vs that range and I have no real redraws. A bluff‑raise OOP is too thin (he can call with many better hands) and I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position with zero showdown value. Fold and preserve my deep stack for better spots.

I'm in UTG (Under The Gun) with 3d Kd, which is a weak hand unsuited with a poor kicker. From early position, I should only be playing premium hands (pairs, AK, AQ, strong broadway combinations) or hands that play well out of position. K3o is well below my UTG opening range, and the disadvantage of acting first pre-flop and post-flop makes this a clear fold. With three opponents still to act behind me, folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack for stronger opportunities. I'm only 1/3 through the tournament with plenty of time to accumulate chips with quality hands.

I'm on the button with Q7s — a playable, suited connector with good positional leverage. Pot is 150 and a simple call costs 100, but opening to 300 (standard ~3x BB) gives me fold equity and a chance to isolate the short-stacked BB (Flash, 21bb) or take the blinds. Elon (SB, deep) can 3-bet, and Q7s is marginal versus a large 3-bet — I'll fold to heavy aggression — but given my large stack (16575) vs the pot, a 300 open is a small, +EV play to maximize steals and play postflop in position.

QJo offsuit is a decent speculative hand with broadway potential and good equity against Minni's likely wide button raising range, as evidenced by her frequent pre-flop raises in previous hands (e.g., Hands 3,7,10,13,15,26,33,34,35,39,42,49). Pot odds are favorable: calling 250 into 450 (plus BB action) requires ~35% equity, and QJo achieves that vs. her range. Out of position, but stacks are deep (my 100+BB, Minni 162+BB), and Flash's short stack (21BB) may create action. Folding is too tight; raising OOP risks playing a marginal hand out of position post-flop. Plan: Call, see flop, c-bet if hit or as bluff if miss, fold to heavy aggression.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Ten offsuit. Minni opened from the Button for 300, and Elon called from the Small Blind. The pot is currently 700, and it costs me 200 to call, giving me pot odds of 3.5-to-1. With a stack of 2100 (21 big blinds), I am the short stack, but QTo is a strong enough hand to defend my blind given the excellent price. I am closing the action pre-flop and can evaluate the board before deciding to commit more of my stack.

Preflop call with QJo was speculative OOP for broadway potential and good equity vs Minni's wide button opening range (frequent raises observed in hands like 49,39,35, etc.). Flop 8d9s3h is excellent: hits precisely with open-ended straight draw (8 outs to 8-9-10-J-Q, ~32% equity by river) plus queen-high blocker/overcard. Rainbow board low, not too drawy. Multiway pot (900) with Flash short-stacked (19BB) and Minni likely c-betting wide (~70% frequency based on history like hand 49 flop bet, hand 38 persistent betting). OOP first to act: betting risks check-raise from Minni's value (sets, two-pair like 98,89) or Flash jam; thin value unlikely as Q-high seldom best. Checking induces c-bet from Minni's range, allows check-raise bluff/semi-bluff if Flash weak or call profitably (pot odds ~3:1 even for turn call), realizes equity passively while controlling pot. Avoid overplaying marginal equity OOP; plan: check-call small/medium bet or check-raise Flash shove/light Minni bet.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Ten offsuit. The flop of 8d-9s-3h does not hit my hand directly, but I do have two overcards to the board. Elon has checked from the Small Blind. As I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Minni), the standard play is to check and see how she proceeds. With a stack of 19 big blinds, I need to be cautious about committing more chips without a made hand or a significant draw.

I’m the preflop raiser (button) with Q♠7♠ on 8♦ 9♠ 3♥. I have no pair, one overcard and only a remote runner-runner spade draw, but I have position and the two opponents checked, showing weakness. This is a good spot to seize initiative with a small c-bet to fold out weak holdings (small pairs, air) and to pressure the short-stack (Flash, ~1.9k). Pot is 900 — a ~1/3-pot bet keeps the pot controlled if called by deep SB (Elon) while providing decent fold equity. If I face significant aggression (a raise), I’ll let this marginal hand go; if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and continue only with improvement or a favorable texture/line. Bet 300 into 900 to take the initiative.

Facing a small 300 bet into 1200 pot (20% pot odds) with QJo holding an open-ended straight draw (8 outs, ~32% equity to river) on a dry 8d9s3h rainbow board. Pot odds require only ~17% equity to call profitably even multiway, and I have far more. Minni's button c-bet is likely wide (~60-70% frequency based on history: frequent flops bets like Hand 49,38,35,15), including many draws, floats, and weak pairs I dominate. Flash's short stack (19BB) checked flop, unlikely to have monster; can fold to aggression or realize equity. OOP, calling keeps pot small, realizes equity passively without bloating vs better hands, sets up turn check-raise if Minni barrels weak or fold to value. Raising semi-bluff risks OOP commitment with reverse implied odds vs sets/two-pair (98,89) and Flash's potential shove; thin value rare with Q-high. Folding marginal equity too tight preflop/call now. Plan: call, evaluate turn board texture; check-fold weak draws if scary card, check-call/raise if clean and Minni weak.

I have Queen-Ten offsuit in the Big Blind. On a flop of 8d-9s-3h, Minni has made a small continuation bet of 300 into a pot of 1200, and Elon has called from the Small Blind. I am being offered pot odds of 5-to-1 (300 to win 1500). With two overcards and a backdoor straight draw, these odds are more than sufficient to see a turn card, especially since my overcards might be the best hand if they hit.

Turn 8h pairs the eights, creating a paired board (8d9s3h8h) with potential two-pair, trips, or full houses now in villains' ranges. QJo holds queen-high with a gutshot straight draw to the 10 (~9% equity to river, 4 outs), no pair, no flush draw, and reverse implied odds OOP multiway. Preflop speculative call and flop call exploited Minni's small c-bet (20% pot), but turn worsens my hand relative to likely holdings: Minni's button range includes 98, 89, 88, 99, sets, draws that hit or improved; Flash's short-stack calling range favors pairs/draws. Pot 1800, OOP first to act: betting as semi-bluff or thin value commits me OOP vs calls/shoves with poor equity/post-oak odds; check allows pot control, realizes fold equity passively, and folding to aggression is +EV as queen-high rarely best (~20-25% vs combined ranges). Minni likely bets wide/strong (history: persistent aggression Hand 38,49), Flash may shove ~16BB. Plan: check-fold to bets/shoves, avoid spew.

On the turn, the board has paired with an eight, and I still have no made hand with my Queen-Ten. Elon has checked from the Small Blind. Given that I am out of position against the original aggressor (Minni) and another player is still in the hand, checking is the most prudent move. My stack is quite low (1600 chips with an 1800 chip pot), so I need to be extremely selective about committing the rest of my chips. I will check and see how Minni acts; if there is significant betting action, I will likely have to fold as I only have overcards.

Turn analysis: board 8♦ 9♠ 3♥ 8♥, my hand Q♠7♠ — no pair and effectively no draw (only runner-runner spade). Both opponents checked the flop and the turn, showing weakness. This is a three-way pot (pot = 1,800) so my double-barrel bluff has limited fold equity and is likely to get called by many hands that currently beat me (9x, any 8, small pairs) or by the short-stacked BB looking to gamble. For value/pressure to be +EV here I’d need a much clearer read or a much narrower field; facing a raise from Elon I’d be forced to fold. I’ll exercise pot control and check back to realize equity and re-evaluate on the river — preserving chips while keeping options open to bluff or take it down if they show further weakness.

River 5s completes no flush (spades draw with 9s5s but board not flushy), doesn't improve my QJo (queen-high, no pair, no straight completed; prior gutshot to T missed). Board 8d9s3h8h5s favors Minni's aggressive button range (frequent PF raises/bets: Hands 49,38,35, etc.) with likely 8x, 98, 89, sets, two-pair, or bluffs/draws that checked turn behind. Flash's short stack (16BB) called flop small bet but checked twice, range capped weak (missed draws/pairs beaten by board). Pot 1800 OOP first to act multiway: value betting impossible (Q-high never best vs calling ranges ~20% equity); bluffing poor (Minni calls wide with medium strength/history of check-calls like Hand 44; Flash shoves light or calls any bet profitably); induces unnecessary action from short stack. Checking maximizes EV: pot control with marginal hand, likely see showdown if checked through (queen occasionally good vs air), fold equity if bet into me; consistent with turn check-fold plan avoiding spew OOP. Preflop/flop calls exploited small bets but no longer justified post-turn pair.

I have Queen-high on a board of 8-9-3-8-5. The pot is 1800 and I have 1600 remaining. On the turn, the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) checked, and Elon has checked the river. While Queen-high has almost no showdown value, bluffing into two opponents who have shown interest in the pot (Minni betting flop, Elon calling flop) is extremely risky, especially since I am the short stack and an all-in would be my only viable bluff size. I will check and hope for a checked-around showdown, though I expect to lose to any pair or Ace-high.

This is a three-way river (8♦ 9♠ 3♥ 8♥ 5♠) and I have Q♠7♠ — no pair and no realistic bluff-outs. Both opponents checked flop/turn/river, which shows either weakness or showdown-ready hands. In a multiway pot my fold equity is very low: any 8, any 9, or most small pairs will call a river bluff, and the short stack (Flash ~1.6k) could jam and force a painful decision. Pot control and chip preservation are the priority with this marginal holding. Betting here is likely -EV, so I check and take it to showdown; if faced with a substantial raise I'll fold.

4-handed table — QTo is a playable opening from UTG. I want to take the initiative, isolate the short SB and deny good odds to the blinds. Standard 3x open is appropriate here (keeps my range wide and applies pressure), so I’ll raise to 300. If I face a small-stack shove from Flash I’ll re-evaluate (I’m prepared to call reasonable shoves), but vs a large 3-bet from Claude or Elon I’ll proceed cautiously and fold marginal holdings. Postflop plan: c-bet favorable textures (Q/T/A-high boards where I connect or have backdoor equity) and give up to heavy, polarized aggression when I miss.

K5s is a marginal suited hand at best, with limited playability post-flop against an UTG raise from Minni, who frequently raises with a strong range. Position on the button is advantageous, but calling 300 into a growing pot risks difficult spots OOP if Flash or Claude squeeze or defend. Folding preserves our deep stack (113x BB) for better spots in this long tournament (51/150 hands), avoiding speculative plays with poor equity realization against a tight opening range. No raise incentive with this hand.

I am currently the short stack with approximately 15.5 big blinds. Minni (UTG) has raised to 3x the big blind. My hand, J3 suited, is too weak to defend from the small blind against a UTG open, especially with my stack size where calling and playing out of position would significantly deplete my remaining chips. Shoving is also not an option with such a weak hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot to commit my remaining 15BB.

I'm holding 2s-6c (the worst hand in poker) in the Big Blind facing a UTG raise from Minni to 300. With only 200 more to call, this might seem like reasonable pot odds (450:200 = 2.25:1), but 2-6o has virtually no equity against an early position raiser. Minni has shown aggressive play from early positions throughout the tournament. This is a clear fold - I cannot profitably call with this garbage hand regardless of pot odds. Preserving my 11,000 chip stack by avoiding marginal/losing situations is the priority when facing 150 hands.

UTG with 8s3d offsuit, one of the worst possible starting hands. No suitedness, no connectivity, low cards with poor playability post-flop. Standard play is to fold pre-flop from early position to preserve stack and wait for better spots. Opponents have deep stacks except Flash who is short, but no reason to enter pot with this hand voluntarily. Folding maximizes EV over long term.

I am on the button with 15.5 big blinds and Ace-Four offsuit. This is a standard opening range hand from this position. Given my short stack relative to the other players, I need to be aggressive to pick up the blinds or double up. I will put in a standard 2.5x raise to 250, which allows me to take down the pot pre-flop while still giving me some flexibility, although I am likely committed to the hand if I see a favorable flop or face a small 3-bet.

Folding 4-5 offsuit from the Small Blind. This is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position against Flash's raise. Although pot odds are reasonable at 2:1, the hand quality is too marginal and I'm vulnerable to Minni's action as well. With 10,950 chips and many hands remaining (52 of 150), I should preserve chips for premium situations.

I’m on the BB with K♦9♥ facing a button open to 250 (I must call 150 into a 400 pot). Flash is very short (~1.3k) and likely opening a wide stealing range. K9o is a defendable hand from the big blind — it can hit top-pair type flops and has reasonable postflop playability. I don’t want to 3-bet large or isolate the short stack with a marginal hand and risk unnecessary commitment; calling keeps the pot controlled and lets me realize equity and reevaluate on the flop. Call 150 (250 total).

I have K♦9♥ on T♥5♦7♣ — just one overcard to the Ten and a backdoor heart, no pair and no strong draw. Flash is short (~13bb) and opened wide from the button, so he will miss often but can also shove. Betting out OOP with a marginal hand risks bloating the pot and facing a shove or getting called by hands that beat me. I prefer pot control and to realize my equity: check. If he checks back I keep the pot small and show down; if he makes a small bet I can call to see a turn with my K-high; if he jams large I can fold and wait for a better spot.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and have been checked to on a relatively dry board of Ten-Seven-Five. While I only have Ace-high, I still hold range advantage as the button raiser. Minni, the big blind, could have many middling hands, but a continuation bet here is standard to fold out her non-pairing overcards like KQ, KJ, or small suited connectors that missed. Given my relatively short stack of 13 big blinds, I want to pick up this pot now without overcommitting. I will bet approximately 40% of the pot. If I am called, I will likely look to check down the turn unless I improve to top pair.

Board: Th 5d 7c. I have K♦9♥. Flash (button) is short (~1.1k) and opened wide preflop, then made a small continuation bet of 225 into a ~775 pot. Folding here is too tight given the pot odds — calling 225 into a ~1000 pot after my call gives ~22.5% break-even equity. K9 has reasonable showdown and improvement equity (one overcard to the Ten, pair/straight outs) versus a wide c-betting range. Raising with a marginal hand is unnecessary and would risk isolating the short stack or bloating the pot; checking/folding surrenders too much. I’ll call to keep the pot controlled, realize equity, and reevaluate on the turn (fold to a large shove/raise if he jams and the turn doesn’t improve me).

I’ll check. I’ve missed — K♦9♥ on Th‑5d‑7c‑Jh has no pair and no realistic flush (max 4 hearts on river), only straight outs (any 8 or Q → 8 outs, ~17% to hit the river). Flash is very short (~1.1k) and opened wide preflop, so any bet here risks either getting shoved on by a short stack or getting called by all of his better hands (T/J/top pairs, middle pairs, pocket pairs). Checking keeps the pot (~1000) under control and lets me realize my equity; I don’t want to manufacture a bluff OOP with almost no fold equity against a short-stacked opponent who can call or jam. If Flash fires a small turn bet I’ll likely call (to see my 17% + pair outs on the river), but I’ll fold to a large shove because K‑high won’t have the required equity. So check and reevaluate on his action or on the river.

On the turn, the Jack of Hearts is a card that likely connects with Minni's flop-calling range (e.g., J-x, 8-9, or J-T). With my Ace-high and a stack-to-pot ratio of approximately 1:1, a bet here essentially commits my remaining chips. Given that Minni called the flop, she likely has at least a piece of the board or a strong draw. By checking back, I preserve my stack for a potential showdown or a better opportunity to commit my chips, while also realizing my equity and seeing a free river card.

Board is Th-5d-7c-Jh-Qc, I hold Kd9h — no pair and poor showdown value. Pot is ~1000 and Flash is very short (~1.1k) who opened wide preflop, small c-bet on the flop and then checked turn. That line is consistent with a lot of missed hands and medium pairs; checking myself will just take us to a showdown where K-high loses most of the time. Betting gives me a chance to fold out his wide checking range (A-highs, floaters, some Kx) and win the pot outright. I don’t want to overcommit with a marginal hand, so I’ll make a half-to-two-thirds pot bluff to maximize fold equity while being prepared to fold to a shove. If he jams I’ll fold K-high; if he calls I accept that I’m likely behind. Bet ~60% pot to credibly represent a Q/J/T and pick up the pot when he missed.

On this board of T-5-7-J-Q, my Ace-high has almost no showdown value. Minni called a pre-flop raise and a flop continuation bet, which strongly suggests she has at least a piece of the board. The turn and river cards (Jack and Queen) are very favorable for a big blind calling range, potentially giving her pairs, two pairs, or even a straight if she held 8-9. Minni's lead for 600 into a 1000 pot is a strong sizing that represents a value hand. With only 1075 chips left, calling away 600 with just Ace-high on such a coordinated board would be disastrous for my tournament life. I will fold and preserve my remaining 10 big blinds for a better double-up opportunity.

I am currently under the gun with 9-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. With only about 11 big blinds left, I am the short stack at the table, but 9-2 is not a hand I can reasonably shove or even open with from this position. I need to preserve my remaining chips for a better hand or a more advantageous position to make a move. Folding is the only viable strategic option here.

I'm on the button with 3-10 offsuit, which is a weak hand that lacks both premium card values and good playability. While position is strong, this hand is below the profitability threshold for a button play. The cost to call is 100 chips, but 3-10o doesn't connect well to most flop textures and is easily outdrawn and dominated by stronger holdings. With 10,950 chips and a healthy stack, I can afford to be selective and wait for better opportunities. Minni in the SB (16,600) and Elon in the BB (11,225) are both competent opponents with decent stacks. The disciplined play is to fold and preserve chips for stronger spots.

I’m in the small blind heads-up vs Elon (big blind) with A♦ K♣ — a premium hand. Stacks are deep (16,600) and a standard, table-consistent open size is about 3x the big blind. I want to build the pot, get value, and apply fold equity to isolate the BB while leaving room to play postflop. Raising to 300 total is the right, standard sizing: it pressures weaker hands, keeps worse hands in that I can extract value from, and sets up a clear plan vs 3-bets (call or 4-bet depending on sizing/read). Action: raise to 300 total.

In the big blind facing a standard small raise to 300 from the small blind after two folds (UTG and Button), the pot is 400 and calling costs 200 more for pot odds of roughly 3:1 (needing about 25% equity). Qc3c is a suited queen, which plays well multiway or with backdoor potential, but here it's heads-up postflop where I have position. Minni's SB opening range after folds is very wide (often stealing), giving Q3s strong equity (~40%+ vs. a loose range). Previous hands show Minni raises frequently from SB but often has showdown value or bluffs postflop. Defending wide in blinds is +EV long-term to maximize stack over 150 hands. Raising risks too much with a marginal hand; calling keeps pot small and realizes equity.

Flop: 5s Ks 4s. I hold Kc Ad — top pair with the nut kicker. Pot is 600 and stacks are deep (effective ~11k). This is a clear value/protection spot: I want to charge worse kings, Kx and Ax hands and punish/price out two-spade combos that have a flush draw (they must have two spades in hand to have a live flush draw on this two-spade flop). A half-pot bet achieves value while keeping the pot manageable with deep stacks. If called I will re-evaluate on the turn and continue extracting/protecting; a large raise would usually indicate a very strong/polarized range (sets/two pair or a rare bluff) and I’ll decide based on sizing. Action: bet for value and protection.

Facing a 300 bet into 900 on a flop of 5s Ks 4s, my Qc3c has completely missed. I have queen-high with no pair, no straight draw (34 is open-ended but needs runner-runner spade-free), and only a weak backdoor club flush draw. The board is extremely draw-heavy with three spades, favoring Minni's wide SB stealing range which includes many spades, kings, sets, or strong draws. Calling OOP keeps me committed to a bluff catcher with poor equity (~20-30% vs her betting range) and risks larger bets on turn/river where position hurts. Raising as a bluff is too thin given her stack (16k) and history of calling stations postflop (e.g., Hand 7, 38). Folding preserves my deep 11k stack for better spots in this long 150-hand tournament, consistent with preflop wide defense but disciplined postflop play. Minni's small bet may induce calls, but equity/playability too poor long-term.

Folding 7d-Jc from UTG. This is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position. From early position, I need stronger holdings to justify entering the pot. With a healthy stack of 10,950, I can afford to be selective and wait for better spots. Position is critical in poker, and UTG is the worst possible position. A weak connector like 7-J is vulnerable to stronger hands and will be difficult to play post-flop if I'm out of position against aggressive opponents like Minni on the button. Clear fold.

4♦9♦ (offsuit) on the button is a very weak hand with poor playability. Although calling 100 is cheap, doing so likely creates a 3‑way pot (SB + short BB) where my hand performs poorly and I lose postflop fold equity. Raising to try and steal risks a shove from the very short-stacked BB (975) or a 3‑bet from Elon (SB), forcing difficult decisions with garbage. With a healthy stack (16950) there's no need to gamble with a marginal hand — preserve chips and wait for a better spot on the button. Fold.

3s4c is a very weak offsuit hand with low cards and poor playability. Preflop from the small blind facing an unopened pot against a short-stacked big blind (975 chips, ~10BB). While stealing blinds is standard in SB vs BB, this hand is in my folding range: bottom ~20% equity even heads-up, terrible postflop OOP if called. Calling/limping risks getting out of position with trash, and raising commits chips with no showdown value. Folding preserves my deep stack (10975) for premium spots over 150 hands. Minni and Claude folded, no multiway concerns.

UTG with Q♠4♥ - clearly a fold. Pot is 150 and I must call 100 (min-raise to 200 available), but Q4o is a weak, frequently dominated hand and plays poorly out of position against the Button, SB and BB. Calling 100 requires ~40% equity to be profitable multiway, which Q4o doesn’t have, and limp/call opens me to a likely shove from the short-stacked SB (Flash 1,075) or strong 3-bet/pressure from the Button/BB. I’ll preserve my 16,950 stack and wait for a better spot.

K3 offsuit is a very weak hand with poor playability, low equity against calling ranges, and dominated by any king or better in opponents' ranges. On the button with blinds to act (short-stacked SB and deep BB), there's no need to get involved voluntarily preflop. Folding preserves stack for better spots in this tournament phase, maximizing long-term EV.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Deuce offsuit. Both the Button and UTG have folded, leaving only the Big Blind, Claude, to act after me. With a stack of approximately 11 big blinds, I need to be aggressive to prevent my stack from dwindling further. An Ace is a strong holding heads-up. I will raise to 300 to exert pressure on the Big Blind and attempt to take the pot pre-flop. If Claude calls, I will have to play carefully out of position, but the strength of the Ace justifies the opening raise.

Pocket 7s is a solid hand in a heads-up situation against Flash's likely wide range from the SB. Flash is short-stacked at 825 and has only 525 remaining after their raise, making them committed. Calling allows me to see a flop with good pot odds and favorable position, while maintaining flexibility on future streets. The wide range from Flash's short-stack push means my pair is likely ahead of their holdings.

I am the pre-flop aggressor with Ace-high, but the flop of Q-9-5 rainbow does not connect with my hand. With a short stack of 825 chips and a pot of 600, I am in a vulnerable position. Betting here would likely commit a significant portion of my remaining stack to a bluff. I will check to see if Claude also missed the board. If he checks back, I get a free turn; if he bets, I will likely fold to preserve my remaining chips for a better opportunity, as Ace-high has low equity against his calling range on this texture.

Betting 400 into the 600 pot applies strong pressure to Flash's 525 chip stack, effectively making this fold-or-commit all-in for him. Pocket 7s is a made hand that's ahead of Flash's likely short-stack raising range. The Queen and 9 on board don't significantly help typical SB shove hands. This bet size is standard aggression that extracts value when Flash shows weakness with his check while maintaining good risk-reward ratio if called.

I started the hand with a short stack and my Ace-high failed to connect with the Queen-high flop. After checking to Claude, he has put out a bet of 400, which is two-thirds of the pot and nearly half of my remaining stack. With only 825 chips left, calling here would leave me with 425 chips and very little equity to improve. To preserve my remaining chips for a more favorable pre-flop shoving opportunity, I must fold this hand.

UTG position with Js3c offsuit is a clear fold. This hand has very poor playability, low equity against ranges, and I'll be out of position post-flop against multiple active opponents. Flash is short-stacked at 825 but that doesn't change the fundamentals here. Preserving stack for premium spots in a long tournament.

I am on the button with a very short stack of 825 chips (8.25 BB). In a 4-handed game, Queen-Eight suited (Q8h) is a strong enough hand to shove from the button to maximize fold equity and seek a double up. Given my stack size, a standard raise would effectively commit me anyway, so shoving 825 is the most mathematically sound move to put pressure on the blinds and potentially pick up the existing pot of 150 chips without a contest.

Folding Ah5h from Small Blind to Flash's button raise. This is a marginal hand that will likely be out of position postflop in a multi-way pot, especially with Minni yet to act and his demonstrated aggressive tendencies. Preserving chips for stronger opportunities is the correct play here.

Flash raised to 825 from the button and Min Raise To: N/A indicates an effective all‑in for ~8.25bb. I have 9♦6♣ in the big blind — a marginal hand but not worthless heads‑up. Calling requires 725 more (825 total) into a 975 pot, giving pot odds ≈42.6% (I need ~43% equity to break even). A button shove this size is typically a wide range, and 9‑6o has roughly that equity versus a wide shove. Raising isn’t meaningful since Flash is effectively all‑in, and my remaining stack is large relative to this risk, so I call to get correct pot odds and defend the blind.

I am currently the short stack with only 17 big blinds (1700 chips). While Ten-Seven of spades is a suited hand with some potential, playing it from under-the-gun (UTG) with such a shallow stack is extremely risky. Raising risks a significant portion of my stack if I'm forced to fold to a 3-bet, and limping is generally a weak play that invites aggression from the larger stacks behind me. Given my tournament position, I should wait for a stronger opening hand or a better position to commit my chips.

I'm holding Td6d on the button. Flash folded, and I'm facing a 100 chip call against Minni (SB) and Elon (BB). While I have positional advantage on the button, Td6d is a weak hand that doesn't warrant playing in a raised pot. The blinds have shown aggression throughout the tournament, and this hand lacks the strength to justify calling a raise out of position relative to the remaining action. With a strong chip position (11200), I can afford to fold marginal hands and wait for better spots. This is a clear fold.

I'm in the small blind heads-up with Qc8h. Heads-up ranges should be wide and I want the initiative — Q8o is good enough to open rather than limp from the SB where I'll be out of position. Table standard opens have been around 3x BB, so I'll raise to 300 total to apply pressure, get folds from worse hands, and keep a playable pot size if called. Deep stack (≈160BB) lets me navigate postflop: continuation-bet on favorable textures (Q/high-card/clear draws) and pot-control or fold to heavy aggression on dry, overcard-heavy boards. If Elon 3-bets large I will fold; if he calls I'll play postflop for position and extract value when appropriate.

Facing a raise to 300 from Minni in the SB after UTG (Flash) and Button (Claude) both folded. Pot is 400, to call 200 more (3:1 odds). My hand is A♦4♦, a suited ace with blockers and flush/straight potential. Minni raises frequently from early positions and blinds based on hand history, suggesting a wide range including Ax, suited connectors, and broadways. A4s has good playability OOP, equity against her range (blocks AA/AK, nut flush draw potential), and excellent implied odds with deep stacks (mine 10k+, hers 15k+). Folding is too tight in BB defense; 3-betting polarizes too much without a strong ace. Calling for pot odds and to realize equity postflop is optimal.

I opened from the SB with Qc8h and Elon just called, so on this flop (2s Qs 5h) I have top pair with a medium kicker. The board is fairly dry (no straight/flush completes) and my preflop-raising range contains a lot of queens and broadways, while his calling range is wide. I want to extract value from worse queens, pocket pairs and small pairs/floaters and deny equity to overcards (A/K) that might float — so a value bet is appropriate. Betting ~1/2 pot is a standard size here: it gets called by worse hands but prices out pure floats. If Elon raises big (polarized) I will seriously re-evaluate and likely fold to very large aggression given my mediocre kicker; I’ll call smaller raises and proceed cautiously on the turn. Betting 300 into the 600 pot balances value and control.

Facing a half-pot continuation bet from Minni on a 2s Qs 5h flop. My Ad4d is ace-high with a diamond backdoor flush draw and backdoor straight potential (wheel or gutshot possibilities). The board is drawy with two spades, favoring her wide SB raising range which includes overcards, Qx, suited broadways, and connectors. I block strong Ax hands like AA/AK/AQ, giving me decent equity (~35-40% vs her polarized cbet range). Pot odds are excellent (900 pot, 300 to call, 3:1), and with deep effective stacks (~10k), implied odds are strong if I connect on turn/river. Folding ace-high OOP is too tight given her frequent aggression shown in history (e.g., Hands 33,35,38 where she cbets light). Raising is suboptimal OOP without a draw to realize fold equity fully. Calling keeps pot small, disguises hand, and allows control postflop while realizing equity.

I have top pair (Qc with an 8 kicker) on a now‑paired board (2s Qs 5h 2c). Elon called my flop c‑bet, so his calling range includes a lot of Qx, some small pairs (5x), and floats — he could have a deuce (flopped trips), but that’s less frequent. I want to extract value from worse queens and small pairs while keeping the pot under control against a possible trips hand. Betting about half the pot gives value and protection without overcommitting; if Elon raises big (polarized) I will fold, whereas I’ll call smaller raises and re‑evaluate on the river. Bet 600 into the 1200 pot to maintain initiative and price in worse hands.

On this paired deuce turn (2s Qs 5h 2c), Minni's 600 bet into 1200 (half-pot) continues her aggression from a wide SB opening range that includes Qx, Ax (which I block), pocket pairs (now possibly improved to two pair or trips), suited connectors, and bluffs/draws. My Ad4d holds ace-high equity (~30-40% vs her polarized range: crushes her bluffs/A-high, live vs Qx/KQ, and has backdoor diamond draw + wheel potential). Pot odds are 3:1 (1800:600, ~25% needed), easily met with direct + implied odds from deep effective stacks (~10k). Folding OOP is too tight given her history of frequent light cbets (e.g., Hands 33,35,38,49,50 where she fires multiple barrels or value bets thinly). Raising OOP risks bloating pot without fold equity against her sticky calling range (as seen vs Elon/Claude), and check-fold was missed. Calling keeps pot controlled, disguises strength, realizes equity, and sets up river decisions with position post-check.

I have top pair (Qc) with an 8 kicker on 2s Qs 5h 2c 7d. Elon called my flop and turn bets, so his range is weighted toward Qx and some small pairs/floaters (with occasional deuces/trips or very rare full houses/quads). The river 7d is mostly a blank and there’s value to extract from worse queens and smaller pairs, while keeping risk manageable against stronger holdings. I’ll make a modest value bet (1,000 into the 2,400 pot) to get called by worse hands but avoid bloating the pot if he has trips; if he raises large I will fold.

River bet of 1000 into 2400 (~42% pot) from Minni after three barrels on a board that paired low deuces (2s Qs 5h 2c 7d). My Ad4d is ace-high with diamond blocker, blocking AA/AK/AQ/ nut diamond flush draws. Her SB opening + cbet range includes Qx (top value), 22 (quads), 55 (two pair), pocket pairs/overpairs turned into value, suited broadways/spade draws (missed), and Ax/connector bluffs. History shows Minni frequently fires multiple light barrels (Hands 33 flop small bet folds, 35 cbet, 38 triple barrel Flash folds river, 49 bet-fold turn). Paired low board caps her value somewhat (no flush, straight unlikely), favors her bluffing missed draws (spade flush draws like AQs/KJs/JTs, wheel draws 34/43). Pot odds 3.4:1 require ~23% equity; I crush bluffs/A-high (~60%+), live vs thin Qx (~33%), fold to monsters. Effective implied/reverse implied minimal with river OOP, but deep stacks (~9k eff) and her sticky history (calls Elon's river bets in 43/44). Raising OOP poor (no fold equity vs calling station tendencies, e.g., Hand 44 river call); check-fold too tight given 3:1 direct odds and blockers. Calling realizes equity vs polarized range (~35-40% overall est.), disguises strength, controls pot size in multiway tourney spot.

Folding 10-4 offsuit from UTG. This hand is well below the opening range for early position. Being first to act with a marginal hand against aggressive opponents (Minni, Elon) in a position where I'll have no initiative post-flop makes this a clear fold. Preserving chips for premium hands is the mathematically correct approach here.

I’m on the button with A♦7♣ — a hand worth opening for a steal. Pot is only 150 and I can apply pressure: Flash (BB) is a short stack (~1.6k) who will either fold or shove; Elon (SB) is deep enough to sometimes defend/3‑bet. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x the big blind) to get fold equity, isolate the short stack, and play in position. If Elon 3‑bets large I’ll proceed cautiously (likely fold A7o); if the short stack shoves I’ll consider calling to isolate since A7o has reasonable equity vs a wide shove and eliminating a short player is valuable. If called and heads‑up, I’ll use positional advantage to continuation‑bet favorable flops and control the pot size on wet boards.

My hand is 3c7s, a complete trash hand with no high card value, no suitedness, and poor playability. Minni raised to 300 from the button, which is a standard open-raise size, but button ranges are wide yet still much stronger than 73o. I'm out of position in the SB facing a likely continuation bettor like Minni, who has been aggressive in previous hands. Defending this wide OOP is -EV; pot odds are about 450:250 or roughly 1.8:1, but equity realization is poor without position or hand strength. Flash is short-stacked in BB but doesn't change my fold. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots.

I am holding 2h 6s in the big blind, which is an extremely weak hand. Minni has raised to 300 from the button. Although I have a short stack of 1600 chips (16 BB) and getting roughly 2.25:1 pot odds to call, playing this hand out of position against an aggressive opponent is likely to lead to further chip loss. I should preserve my remaining stack for a better opportunity or a hand with better equity.

UTG with K2 of hearts (K2s) is marginal, but with a deep stack (18475, ~185bb) and three players to act I want to take the initiative and avoid a multiway limp. A standard 3x open to 300 preserves my image (I’ve been opening to 300 frequently), gives fold equity against the blinds (especially the short SB of 1550 who may fold or shove light), and allows me to play position/initiative postflop. Min-raising to 200 is too small and inviting; limp/call is too passive. Plan: open to 300, c-bet favorable flops but proceed cautiously with K‑x and a weak kicker; fold to large 3‑bets unless the sizing and reads justify a call.

Pocket 9s is a premium hand, roughly top 10% equity preflop, performing well against UTG opening ranges. Minni's UTG raise suggests a strong but polarized range including pairs, broadways, and suited connectors. As button, I have excellent position on all players postflop. 3-betting isolates Minni, builds the pot with my strong hand, denies equity to worse hands, and applies pressure on the short-stacked SB (Flash, 1550 chips) who may fold or shove predictably. Calling is suboptimal due to multiway pot risk with worse hands; folding is too tight. Standard 3-bet size vs UTG open is 3x (900), which is pot-committing-ish for SB but balanced. Larger sizes risk overcommitting OOP stacks. Plan: 3-bet to 900, proceed cautiously postflop based on board and action.

With a stack of 15.5 BBs, I am the short stack at the table. In this hand, Minni raised from UTG and Elon 3-bet from the Button. Qs Tc is a marginal hand that is likely dominated by the ranges of both the initial raiser and the 3-bettor. Calling away more than half of my stack would leave me in a terrible position post-flop, and shoving over the top is too risky given the strength shown by my opponents. I will fold and wait for a better spot to commit my remaining chips.

Q5o is too weak to continue against Elon's 3-bet from button. I have insufficient equity (~20-25%) relative to pot odds required (26%), I'm out of position, and my stack is healthy enough to fold marginal hands. Standard fold in this spot.

Elon 3‑bet to 900 from the button after my UTG open to 300. That sizing + position strongly indicates a range containing many hands that dominate K2s (Ax/Kx and broadways) and he will have positional advantage postflop. K2h is a marginal, poorly playing hand OOP with a weak kicker and few clean ways to realize equity; the ~3.25:1 pot odds on a call don’t overcome the domination and postflop disadvantage. A minimum 4‑bet to 1500 would be a bad bluff with this holding (no useful blockers and likely to be called/raised). I give up the 300, fold this spot, and wait for a better, less dominated spot to invest chips.

UTG position with 3c Ts offsuit, an extremely weak hand with poor playability, no high card strength, and bad post-flop potential. Standard pre-flop strategy dictates folding trash like this from early position to avoid difficult situations out of position and preserve stack for premium hands. Pot is small (150), to-call only 100 but committing chips with this hand is -EV long-term in a tournament setting.

I am currently the short stack with 15.5 big blinds. In a 4-handed game, Ace-Five offsuit is a strong hand from the button. At this stack depth, an open-shove is a standard and effective strategy to maximize fold equity and avoid difficult post-flop decisions against the larger stacks in the blinds (Claude and Minni). By going all-in, I force them to have a premium hand to call, while preventing them from realizing equity with marginal hands that might outdraw me. This is the best move to either take down the blinds and antes or double up.

With 5d-Qc from small blind, this is a marginal hand facing a button raise to 1,550 from Flash. While I have some showdown value with queen-high, this hand lacks strong equity against a positional raiser. Given my mid-stack position (11,050 chips) and the fact that the big blind (Minni) hasn't acted yet, folding preserves my chips for stronger opportunities. This is a disciplined fold—I'm already committed for 50 chips as small blind, and calling 1,500 more commits nearly 14% of my stack with a weak holding that will likely miss the flop. In a high roller tournament, selectivity from early positions is crucial, and 5-Q offsuit doesn't merit this investment.

Tc6s is a weak, offsuit, non‑connecting hand that will be heavily dominated by a BTN open. Flash raised to 1550 (pot 1700) — I must call 1450 (~14.5bb). Break‑even equity on a call is about 1450/(1700+1450) ≈ 46%. Tc6o does not have anywhere near that vs a BTN opening range, and I’ll be out of position postflop. 3‑betting as a bluff with this hand is poor (few useful blockers, little playability). With a healthy stack and many hands left in the tournament, the correct play is to conserve chips and fold.

I am currently the short stack with 17 big blinds (1700 chips). In a 4-handed game, an Ace-high hand like A5o is strong enough to open. Given my stack size, a standard raise to 2.5x or 3x would commit nearly 20% of my stack and leave me in a difficult position post-flop out of position against the larger stacks. Shoving all-in (17 BBs) maximizes my fold equity, ensures I realize all my equity, and prevents the opponents from out-maneuvering me on the flop. Since I won the previous hand with a large raise, I will maintain that pressure to rebuild my stack.

Flash is all-in for 1700 from UTG with his remaining stack. While the pot odds (1850 to 1700) look somewhat attractive on the surface, I need to decline with 9-7 offsuit. This is a weak hand with approximately 30-35% equity at best against a random range, and I still have Minni (SB) and Elon (BB) to act behind me. If either of them re-raises or gets involved, I'd be folding anyway in a worse spot. I have a solid 11,050 chip stack and maintain good positioning in the tournament (hand 61 of 150). Discipline dictates folding marginal hands from weaker positions against all-in threats, especially when I can wait for stronger opportunities. The pot odds don't compensate for the hand weakness and positional vulnerability here.

Flash (UTG) has shoved all-in to 1700 leaving 0; I’ve posted the small blind (50) so calling costs me 1650. My hand J♣6♥ is weak and is dominated by a large portion of an UTG shove range (pairs, broadways, A/Kx). If Elon (BB) elects to call we’ll be in a multiway pot where my already-poor equity falls even further. Pot odds: I’d need roughly 47% equity to break even on a call (1650 / (1850+1650) ≈ 47%), which J6o does not have against a reasonable UTG shove, and multiway it’s worse. I have a healthy stack (18k) so there’s no need to gamble with a marginal holding — preserve chips and wait for a better situation. Fold.

Flash shoved all-in UTG for 1700 (about 17BB), which indicates a strong premium range like AA, KK, QQ, AK, JJ+. My 5h6s is a speculative offsuit connector with poor equity heads-up against this range, likely around 25-30% at best. Pot odds are roughly 1.15:1 (1600 to call into 1850 pot), requiring over 46% equity to break even on average, which I don't have. Folding preserves my healthy 9075 stack for better spots in a tournament with 150 hands total. No need to gamble here.

I'm holding J4o (Jack of Clubs, Four of Clubs) from UTG (first to act), which is one of the worst positions at the table. This is a weak hand with limited potential - it's not a pocket pair, not a strong broadway card, and the four kicker plays poorly. From UTG, standard poker strategy requires playing only premium hands (high pocket pairs, AK, AQ, etc.). J4 doesn't meet this threshold. Being out of position with a marginal hand would put me at a significant disadvantage throughout the hand. Flash is short-stacked at 1,750, but that doesn't change the fact that I need a stronger hand to play from UTG. I have a healthy stack (11,050) and can afford to wait for better spots. Folding is the mathematically correct play here.

I’m on the button with Kd7c — a marginal hand but with strong positional value and a King blocker. UTG has folded so I can pressure just the blinds. Flash (BB) is short (~17BB) and will often fold or shove; Elon (SB) is deep and capable of 3-bets but also defends. I want to open a wide stealing range from the button while keeping the pot manageable, so I’ll make a small standard raise to 2x the big blind (200 total). This maximizes fold equity against the blinds and preserves post‑flop options. If Elon 3-bets big I’ll fold K7o; I’ll evaluate any short-stack shove from Flash based on exact sizing and pot odds.

Q7o is a marginal offsuit hand, not well-suited for calling a raise out of position from the small blind. Minni's button raise suggests a wide range, but it still dominates Q7o much of the time (better queens, etc.). Pot odds to call 150 into 350 (~2.3:1) are decent, but implied odds are poor OOP with this hand lacking playability, straight potential, or suitedness. Folding preserves my deep effective stack (9025) for premium spots in this multi-table dynamic. Flash in BB with 1750 may defend or squeeze, but I don't need to get involved here. Standard fold to maximize long-term EV.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Trey offsuit and have been offered excellent pot odds (3.5:1) to defend against a button min-raise from the chip leader. My stack is currently 1750 (17.5 BBs). While this is a common stack size for a 3-bet shove against a wide button opening range, I have played the previous two hands very aggressively by raising large pre-flop. Shoving for a third consecutive hand might induce a call from Minni with a wider range of hands that could dominate my weak kicker (like A7+ or small pairs). By calling the 100 chips, I see a flop for a very small investment, preserving my stack's utility while allowing me to evaluate the board. If I hit an Ace or a favorable draw, I can look to commit my stack then, but for now, I will take the profitable price to see three cards.

I have missed the flop completely with my Ace-Trey offsuit on a board of King-Ten-Six. This board texture favors the pre-flop aggressor (Minni), as it hits many of the broadway cards and pairs in a button opening range. With only 16.5 BBs remaining, I cannot afford to peel light or make moves without significant equity. My plan is to check and, in most scenarios, fold to a continuation bet, as I lack any straight or flush draws and my Ace-high has low showdown value against her range here.

I raised preflop on the button and now have top pair (Kd) on Kc–Td–6d. Flash (BB) is short (~16–17BB) and checked — a classic spot to c‑bet for value and protection. My hand has good showdown value and blocks some Kx combos; a bet charges broadway/straight draws (QJ, J9, etc.), extracts value from worse Ks, Ts and middle pairs, and makes draws pay to continue. I’ll size to ~2/3 pot to both get value and deny correct odds to many draws while keeping the pot manageable against his short stack. If he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn; if he shoves I’m willing to call given my equity vs a short‑stack shove range.

I missed the flop entirely with my Ace-Trey offsuit. The board (King-Ten-Six) is very favorable for Minni's button opening range, which likely contains many Kings, Tens, and broadway draws. With my current stack at 16.5 BBs, I need to be selective about which pots I contest. Calling here with just Ace-high and no significant draws would be a waste of chips. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to commit my remaining stack.

Short-handed (4 players) so ranges should be wider — A5s is a standard open in this spot. I have deep stack (~183bb) while Flash (SB) is short (~16bb) and Claude (BB) and Elon (BTN) are both deep-ish. Limping/calling UTG invites multiway pots with poor equity and reduces my ability to steal. Raising gives fold equity, isolates the short stack, and lets me play the hand aggressively postflop. I choose a standard 3x open to 300: big enough to apply pressure and discourage limp/calls, but not so large that I unduly bloat the pot or invite oversized 3-bets. Plan contingencies: call a shove from Flash (A5s is reasonably strong vs a short-shove range); vs a large 3-bet from Elon or Claude I will re-evaluate (often fold out of position unless the 3-bet is small or I get proper pot odds). If called, play the flop aggressively when favorable and use backdoor nut/flush potential.

Qd2s is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability and low equity against an UTG raiser like Minni, who has been aggressive in previous hands. In a 4-handed game, Button position allows wider ranges, but calling 300 into a pot of 450 with this hand is -EV due to domination risks and lack of postflop potential. Folding preserves my 9025 stack for better spots. No 3-bet value or bluff equity here given Minni's stack and tendencies.

I am currently the short stack with 16 big blinds. Holding Queen-Jack suited (Qh Jh) in the Small Blind is a very strong hand in a 4-max game. Minni, who is the chip leader, has been opening a wide range of hands from all positions. While an all-in shove is a mathematically sound strategy to capitalize on fold equity, calling allows me to see a flop with a hand that has excellent playability and post-flop potential. Given my stack size, I will call the 250 chips to see the flop, while remaining prepared to commit the rest of my chips on a favorable board.

Calling with K♠8♠ in the big blind. Getting 4.5:1 pot odds which justifies seeing a flop with a hand that has top pair and flush draw potential. The multiway pot and decent stack size make this a standard defensive big blind call rather than a fold or aggressive raise.

On a board of Ah-Kc-5c, I have Queen-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a Ten) and a backdoor heart flush draw. I am out of position against two opponents, one of whom was the pre-flop raiser (Minni). This board texture strongly favors the UTG raiser's range, which contains many Aces and Kings. Given my short stack (13.5 BB), I need to be careful. Leading out here as a bluff is unlikely to work against two players on such a high-card board. Checking is the most prudent move to see how the action develops and whether I can get a cheap card to hit my straight.

I have K♠8♠ on a 5♣ A♥ K♣ flop, which gives me top pair with a strong flush draw (4 spades to the flush). Flash checked weakness from the small blind with a short stack (1,350), and Minni is the pre-flop aggressor from UTG. This is a favorable spot to bet for value. I'm not facing a raise yet, and the 900-chip pot justifies a 500-chip bet to build value, generate folds from marginal hands, and get heads-up or 3-way value from Minni. The flush draw provides excellent equity even if called, and I have sufficient chips to handle a raise. This is standard value betting with a strong hand that has additional draw potential.

Flop: 5c Ah Kc — I hold As5s: two pair (Aces and Fives). Claude’s bet 500 into a 900-pot (pot now 1400) is standard for a c-bet/continuation or a value bet, but my two-pair is ahead of most of his continuing range (most A-x, K-x, single pairs, and club draws). Only a limited number of combos beat me (pocket Aces, AK, pocket Kings, pocket Fives — roughly ~11 combos), so this is a clear spot to build the pot and protect. I want to charge club draws and get value from Ax and Kx that will call a sizable raise, and I don’t want to give a free card. With deep effective stacks I’ll raise for value and to deny equity to draws. I choose a pot-sized raise to 1900 total on the flop (raise action), which pressures draws, extracts value from worse A/K hands, and keeps decision-making straightforward if Flash (short) shoves or Claude commits more — I’ll be willing to call a reasonable shove given the hand’s strength.

On a flop of Ace-King-Five, my Queen-high hand has very little equity against two opponents who are both showing significant strength. Claude bet into the pre-flop raiser, and Minni (the original raiser) responded with a large raise to 1900. My gutshot straight draw (needing a Ten) is only a four-outer, and given that the pot is now being contested heavily on a board that perfectly hits an UTG and Big Blind's calling range, I am almost certainly crushed. Calling here would commit my entire remaining stack of 1350 chips with a very low probability of winning. I must preserve my remaining chips for a better opportunity.

Calling Minni's 1900 raise with K♠8♠ on a 5♣ A♥ K♣ flop. I have top pair and a strong 11-card spade flush draw. Against Minni's likely AK (two pair), I have approximately 40% equity, which exceeds the 31% needed given 2.25:1 pot odds. My draw provides multiple ways to win on the turn or river, and my stack size (10,250) comfortably supports this call. Folding top pair with this much draw potential would be unnecessarily cautious in this high roller format.

Checking on the turn with K♠8♠ (top pair, weak kicker) on a 5♣ A♥ K♣ 7♠ board. After calling Minni's aggressive flop raise of 1900, the 7♠ turn doesn't significantly strengthen my hand - I can no longer make a flush by the river (would need 2 more spades). My draw-heavy equity rationale from the flop is no longer valid. With limited outs and a vulnerable pair, I should check and cede control to Minni, allowing me to reassess on her turn action. This preserves chips while keeping the hand alive, and gives me information about her hand strength.

I have As5s — two pair (Aces & Fives) on 5c Ah Kc 7s. Claude bet the flop, I raised to 1,900 and he called; he now checks the turn. Heads-up my two-pair is very strong and only a small portion of his continuing range (AK, KK, 55) actually beats me. The turn is a relatively harmless 7s but it can give some club/runner draws a free card if I check. I want to both extract value from Kx and worse A-x hands and deny equity to club draws, so I’ll bet for value and protection. The pot is 4,700 — I choose a ~60–70% pot-sized bet to charge worse hands while keeping options flexible against a raise. If he calls I still expect to be well ahead; if he raises big I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing (large raises polarize to sets/AK and will often require folding or a careful call). Bet 3,000.

Folding K♠8♠ on the 5♣ A♥ K♣ 7♠ turn against Minni's aggressive three-street action. My pair is weak due to the Ace on board and weak 8 kicker. My draw equity is gone (would need running cards for flush). Minni's pattern (UTG raise → flop raise to 1900 → turn raise to 3000) indicates significant strength, likely AK for two pair.
