Checking keeps the pot under control, lets me see a cheap river, and preserves the ability to fold to real aggression. If Claude bets the river I’ll fold most large bets and consider calling smaller ones or value-betting if I pair up. If he checks back the river I can try a targeted value bet when I improve or bluff-catch selectively. Most EV-maximizing line here is to check behind.

Betting 500 on the turn with top pair and a strong kicker. Minni's two checks indicate weakness, and this is a clear value situation. The sizing is appropriately aggressive without overcommitting - roughly 40% of the pot. This extracts value from weaker holdings (flush draws, weaker kings, small pairs) while maintaining proper pot odds and position advantage in heads-up play.

I checked the turn with QcTh and Claude bet 500 into a 1,200-pot (1700 total). I missed — my backdoor club draw is dead and I only have ~6 outs (Qs or Ts) ≈13% to improve on the river. Calling 500 to win 2,200 (pot after call) requires ~22.7% equity, so the raw draw math is negative. Claude is the preflop raiser and his line (flop c‑bet then a turn continuation) is consistent with Kx or a controlled value/pressure line; raising as a bluff out of position would be too risky with little fold equity. Per my earlier plan to defend small but fold to significant turn aggression, folding preserves stack and avoids a marginal, -EV call.

Heads-up on the button with As2d — any Ace is a premium heads-up holding and I have position postflop. I want to take the initiative and apply pressure, so I’ll open-raise to the min (2x BB = 200 total). That sizing gives good fold equity while keeping the pot manageable with a weak kicker. If Claude folds I pick up the blinds; if he calls I’ll play postflop in position (strong equity to hit top pair / use the Ace blocker). If he 3-bets I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and tendencies (call small 3-bets to see a flop, fold or 4-bet for larger confrontations). Overall this is a standard aggressive heads-up open to maximize chip accumulation.

Calling with K5o from the big blind in heads-up play. Excellent 3:1 pot odds (calling 100 to win 300), decent hand strength with a strong kicker, and positional advantage post-flop. Standard big blind defense with a marginal hand given the favorable odds and likely wide raising range from Minni in heads-up play.

Checking with K5o on 7d 8c 2c in heads-up from out-of-position. King-high has marginal strength with no draws. Checking accomplishes pot control, keeps my range balanced, and allows me to evaluate Minni's flop action before committing further chips with a weak hand. This is standard defensive play with a vulnerable holding while out of position.

Flop: 7d 8c 2c — I have As2d: a pair of deuces with the Ace blocker and I’m in position. Heads-up, bottom/mid pair is often worth betting for value and protection. A half-pot c-bet (~200 into 400) charges club draws, folds out many overcards and floaters, and extracts value from worse pairs and Ax/Kx hands that call pre. Size keeps the pot manageable; if Claude calls I’ll reassess on the turn, and if he check-raises big I’ll give significant credit to a 7/8/sets and fold or proceed cautiously depending on sizing (small check-raise I may call, large I’ll most likely fold).

Calling with K5o on 7d 8c 2c. Pot odds are 3:1, favorable enough for a marginal hand with some showdown value in heads-up play. Minni's flop c-bet is typical and my king-high maintains equity against a wide range. Calling allows me to evaluate the turn card while avoiding overcommitting with a weak holding out of position.

King-high with the 3d turn card is a marginal hand at best. I have no draw, no showdown value improvements, and I'm out of position. The turn card didn't improve my hand at all - I still just have K-high. Checking accomplishes several things: (1) it keeps the pot manageable with a weak holding, (2) it allows me to see what Minni does on a turn that doesn't help either of us (presumably), and (3) it avoids bloating the pot with a marginal holding from out of position. In heads-up play, Minni has been consistently aggressive with c-bets, but checking here is the prudent line - if Minni bets, I can re-evaluate based on pot odds and her betting pattern. With just king-high and no draws, I'm looking to keep this pot under control rather than put more chips at risk.

Heads‑up I have As2 and just improved to second pair on the turn (board 7♦ 8♣ 2♣ 3♦). Claude checked the turn after calling my half‑pot c‑bet on the flop, which signals weakness or a marginal holding/draw. Against his calling range I can extract value from A‑high hands, Kx/Qx, and small pairs while charging missed clubs and denying a free river. A half‑pot bet keeps the pot manageable vs sets/strong two‑pairs but gives good fold equity against overcards and floats. Plan: bet for value/protection; if he flats I’ll re‑evaluate river; if he check‑raises large I’ll give credit to a stronger made hand and likely fold. Betting 400 is the right balance here.

Calling with K5o on turn with 4:1 pot odds. King-high has marginal strength but sufficient equity (~30-35%) against Minni's wide heads-up range to justify the favorable odds. The turn 3d didn't improve my hand, so I'm not committing additional chips beyond the call. This defensive play from out of position allows me to evaluate river action while maintaining a balanced range. Standard big blind defense in heads-up with adequate pot odds.

River with K5o on 7d 8c 2c 3d 7s leaves me with just king-high and a paired board - an extremely weak holding. Checking accomplishes critical objectives: (1) controls pot size with insufficient hand strength, (2) avoids overcommitting chips from out of position, (3) allows evaluation of Minni's river action without further risk, and (4) may result in showdown if she checks. With no draws and marginal hand strength, betting is illogical. Checking is the defensive, chip-preserving play that balances my range while keeping the pot under control.

I have As2d and the river (7♦ 8♣ 2♣ 3♦ 7♠) gives me two pair — sevens and deuces. Claude has check‑called my flop and turn bets, which looks like a fairly sticky, medium‑strength calling range (8x, small pairs, some 3x/2x) but only a small portion of his range contains a 7 (trips/full house). Checking behind surrenders thin value to many worse hands. I’ll make a value bet to extract from those worse one‑pair and two‑pair/ax hands while keeping the size small enough to limit loss to the relatively rare trip/full‑house combos. Plan: bet ~½ pot — 800 chips. If he flats we go to showdown; if he raises big I’ll give him credit for trips/full house and fold to large aggression.

River fold with K5o on 7d 8c 2c 3d 7s. I have king-high with a paired board - an extremely weak hand. While pot odds are favorable at 3:1 (needing ~25% equity), my hand strength doesn't support a call here.

Key factors in this decision:
1. Hand strength: K-high is the worst holding, and the river 7 paired the board without helping me
2. Betting pattern: Minni has consistently bet flop (200), turn (400), and now river (800), suggesting strength rather than bluffing
3. River dynamics: The paired 7 is likely to have improved her range significantly more than mine
4. Position: I'm out of position and checking throughout signals weakness
5. Range analysis: Against Minni's likely holdings (any pair, 8x, 7x combos), I'm in bad shape

While I called down through the flop and turn with marginal pot odds in heads-up play (standard big blind defense), the river is a different decision. The progression of betting, combined with the board pairing and my complete lack of showdown value, makes this an easy fold. Even at 3:1 odds, I need roughly 25% equity - and against an opponent who's been consistent with her betting, K-high doesn't meet that threshold.

Fold preserves chips for better spots ahead.

Raising to 250 (2.5x big blind) with Jd-As in heads-up position. J-A offsuit is a premium hand that should be played aggressively from the button. Raising applies pressure, wins the pot sometimes pre-flop, and ensures I'm playing the hand in position when called. This sizing is standard aggression - strong but not overly committal given the stack depths.

Heads-up vs the button who opened to 250 from the small blind. I’m in the big blind with Q♦T♦ — a strong, playable hand that does very well both as a preflop 3-bet and postflop with lots of nutted/semibluff equity. With deep stacks (≈22100 vs 17550) I want to seize initiative and get fold equity against a likely wide opening range. A 3-bet to 900 (well above the 400 minimum) pressures weaker opens while leaving room to maneuver postflop. If Claude folds I scoop the 350 pot; if he calls the pot will be ~1800 and I’ll proceed with a c-bet on favorable flops or play my draws/top pairs aggressively. If he 4-bets large I’ll tighten up and fold most non-premiums. Overall this sizing balances fold equity and postflop playability and maximizes EV heads-up.

Calling Minni's 3-bet with J-A offsuit is the right play in heads-up. While J-A isn't premium, it's definitely strong enough to not fold to a single re-raise. In HU play with wider ranges, this hand has solid equity. A 4-bet would commit too much with a hand that whiffs flops frequently. Calling 650 more (900 total) keeps the SPR reasonable and allows me to play well post-flop. I'll have positional information on the flop despite being out of position preflop.

I have Qd with Qc Qs on the flop — trips (top trips), an extremely strong hand heads-up. Claude called my 3-bet preflop, so his calling range is wide (some Qx, pocket pairs like 88/99/TT, broadways and club combos). I want to extract value from worse hands and induce calls from club draws and medium pairs without folding them out, so I’ll make a small-to-medium value bet (~1/3 pot). Betting 600 into the 1800 pot maximizes calls from worse hands while keeping future barrels profitable; if he raises I can commit or re-evaluate knowing I’m well ahead.

Calling 600 with J-A on a paired queen board against Minni's flop bet. I have approximately 30-35% equity against a wide HU 3-bet range, exceeding the 20% pot odds required. Six clean outs (three Jacks, three Aces) provide reasonable equity, and the call keeps the SPR manageable while allowing me to play well post-flop. Folding J-A here would be too weak in heads-up play where opponent ranges are wide.

Board is Qc Qs 8c 9c, pot 3000. I have QdTd — top trips with a medium kicker. The turn completes a three-club board, so the only hands that now beat me are two-club combos that made a club flush (both hole cards clubs) and the lone remaining queen (Qh) which would tie. Claude called my flop lead, so his range contains lots of Qx, pocket pairs (88,99,TT+), and some two-club combos that were drawing on the flop. I want to extract value from worse Qx and pocket pairs and charge any single-club hands, while not folding out marginal callers. Betting also denies a free river that could pair the board and give lower sets/full houses more equity.

I’ll make a sizable value bet but not overbet the pot — 2/3 pot (2000). This price gets calls from Qx, 88, 99, KQ/AQ type hands and smaller pairs, charges club draws, and keeps the initiative. If Claude raises very large (polarized), it’s likely a made club flush and I’ll have to re-evaluate (I may fold to an extreme overbet depending on sizing, call a smaller raise, or commit if the raise commits most of his stack). If he calls, I’ll reassess on the river for further value or pot-control.

Calling 2000 on the turn with J-As against an aggressive opponent. I need ~28.5% equity to break even, and against Minni's likely range (QQ, AQ, KQ, semi-bluffs), I have approximately 24-30% equity with 6 outs. In heads-up play, this is a reasonable spot to continue with a premium hand that hasn't improved yet. The pot odds are acceptable, I have one full river card to come, and folding J-A would be overly cautious in this dynamic. I maintain optionality to fold on the river if necessary.

Board: Qc Qs 8c 9c 8d. My hand Qd Td = Queens full of eights (QQQ88) — essentially the nuts except for quads 8s (only pocket 88 beats me). Claude’s line (call 3-bet pre, call 600 on flop, call 2000 on turn) is wide and includes pocket pairs, Qx, and club combos; most of that range is behind my full house or will at best tie. Checking surrenders value to worse hands (club combos, single Q combos that now tie, some pocket pairs) so I should value-bet. I’ll choose a moderate size (~1/2 pot) to extract from worse hands while not pricing them out and while allowing me to fold to a highly polarized check-raise that would strongly indicate quads. Pot = 7000; bet 3500 is a balance between extracting value and avoiding overbetting into the tiny quads frequency.

Calling 3500 on the river with Jd-As (one pair, AJ kicker). Pot odds are 4:1, requiring only 20% equity. Against Minni's aggressive betting line, my hand has approximately 22-26% equity, accounting for value hands I beat/tie with and potential bluffs. In heads-up play with a wide opponent range, J-A high is strong enough to call with these favorable odds. Previous folds in similar spots would be too cautious given the dynamic.

Heads-up on the button with 8♣3♦ — a very weak offsuit hand, but heads-up opening ranges should be wide. Pot is 150, cost to call 50 and min-raise to 200 (2x BB). I’ll raise to 200 to take the initiative, seize fold equity from the big blind, and use position postflop to realize equity or fold cheaply when I miss. Effective stacks: my 29,150 vs his 10,700 (deep enough to play postflop), so a small open is low-risk and keeps options flexible. If Claude 3-bets large I’ll fold this hand; if he calls I’ll proceed in position and c-bet or control the pot depending on the flop.

Heads-up play against aggressive Minni. 7-9 offsuit is marginal but playable with 3:1 pot odds. Can make straights and pairs. Stack disadvantage means I should selectively enter pots with hands that have potential. This is a reasonable call to see a flop against button aggression.

Holding 7♠9♣ on a flop of A♣4♠J♥ gives me just a gutshot straight draw (only 8 outs to make 7-8-9). This is a weak holding with minimal equity. I'm heads-up against aggressive Minni with a significant stack disadvantage (10.6k vs 29k), so I need to be selective about committing chips. By checking, I allow Minni to show her hand strength. If she checks back, I have a free card and can reassess on the turn. If she bets, I'll have information to make a better decision about whether to call, fold, or raise based on pot odds and her tendencies. This cautious approach preserves my stack while gathering information in a marginal situation.

Heads-up after my preflop open I should be c-betting a high frequency — Claude’s cold‑call range is wide and misses this A♣4♠J♥ flop a lot. I have nothing (8♣3♦) and only weak backdoors, so I want a small, high‑EV probe to take the initiative and fold out unpaired broadways/small pairs while keeping the pot controlled. I’ll make a small continuation bet (~1/3 pot = 150). Plan: fold to a strong check‑raise, but if called I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn and usually give up unless I pick up real equity or see a good bluffing opportunity.

Folding 7♠9♣ on A♣4♠J♥ against Minni's flop bet. While I have a gutshot straight draw (needing 5, 6, or 8), this hand lacks sufficient equity. I'm getting ~3.67:1 pot odds (550:150), but my gutshot draw has only ~25-30% equity against Minni's likely strong holdings given she raised preflop and followed up with a bet on a flop that whiffed my hand. Being out of position, having a significant stack disadvantage (10.6k vs 28.85k), and facing an aggressive opponent all argue for folding weak draws. In a deep-stacked tournament against an opponent who has shown consistent aggression, I need to be selective about when I commit chips. This is not a spot worth defending with marginal equity. Preserving my stack for stronger opportunities is the correct long-term strategy.

I'm on the button (small blind) heads-up against Minni with J9d. I only need to call 50 more chips, making this an easy decision. J9 is a solid hand in heads-up play, especially with diamond suited cards that give me backdoor flush potential. With my shorter stack (10,550 vs Minni's 29,300), I need to play poker and accumulate chips when I have playable hands. Folding too much will blind down quickly. Calling to see a flop is the standard play here.

Heads-up vs a limp from the small blind — Claude has a very wide range. 2♠3♥ is too weak to profitably raise or lead out here (very poor equity vs his calling range and little fold equity). Pot is small (200) and my stack is deep, so I’ll check and see a flop, then fold to meaningful pressure unless I hit. Preserves stack and lets me realize equity or take a free card.

Check. Heads-up to a limp, Claude has a very wide range and 2♠3♥ has almost no equity vs his calling range — no backdoor flush and only six weak pair outs. The pot is tiny (200) and my stack is deep, so I want to realize equity without bloating the pot. I’ll check to see a turn and only continue if I improve (pair 2 or 3) or if he gives a very cheap card; otherwise fold to meaningful pressure.

J9d on Td 4d 7s is a strong semi-bluff in heads-up. I have a flush draw (9 outs), straight draw potential (K or 6), and overcards. Minni checked the flop showing weakness, which is exploitable. With a short stack (10,500), I need to accumulate chips with playable hands. A 100-chip bet takes advantage of position, applies pressure, and maintains fold equity while having solid equity if called. This is standard aggressive heads-up poker in chip accumulation mode.

Heads-up vs a limp, Claude has a very wide range. My 2♠3♥ has virtually no backdoor draws on T♦4♦7♠ — only six outs (three 2s + three 3s) to pair by river (~24% equity). Claude's bet is small (100 into a 300 pot) so the immediate pot‑odds are about 20% (break‑even), meaning a call is marginally +EV on raw equity. Raising here with no fold equity or folding a cheap decision would be too exploitable. Plan: call 100 to see the turn; if I miss I'll fold to meaningful turn aggression unless I pick up a pair or a very cheap card to realize further equity.

Check. I have 2♠3♥ on T♦4♦7♠5♠ — no made hand and no realistic flush (with only one spade in my hand and two on board a river spade still can’t make a five‑card flush). My clean outs are pairing a 2 or 3 (3 twos + 3 threes) or completing the wheel with an A or a 6 (4 aces + 4 sixes) — 14 raw outs ≈ 30% to improve on the river, but many of those will still lose to a made ten/boat/set. Claude’s line (SB limp then a small flop bet) is very wide, but betting now would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and deny me the chance to realize equity cheaply. Pot is small (400) and my stack is deep, so I’ll check to see the river; if I miss I’ll fold to significant river aggression but will call small river bets that give correct pot odds, and if I pair/complete the straight I’ll look to extract value.

Turn semi-bluff with strong draw. I have J9d on Td 4d 7s 5s with ~13 clean outs (flush draw, straight draws, overcards) = roughly 52% equity. Minni checked weakness. With my short stack (10,400), I need to be aggressive with playable hands and build pots. A 250-chip bet applies pressure, builds pot equity, and may win immediately. This is standard aggressive heads-up play in chip accumulation mode.

Heads‑up vs a very wide SB limp/c-bet line. I hold 2♠3♥ on T♦‑4♦‑7♠‑5♠ — no made hand but 14 clean river outs (3 twos + 3 threes + 4 aces + 4 sixes) ≈ 14/46 ≈ 30.4% to improve. The pot is 650 and Claude’s raise requires 250 to call, so the break‑even equity is ~250/(650+250)=27.8%. My raw equity (~30%) is slightly above the break‑even threshold, so a call is +EV. Many of the outs (pairing 2/3) can still be second‑best vs a turned pair/top pair, but Claude’s line (limp pre, small flop bet, modest turn shove) represents a very wide range including bluffs and marginal hands — and this is a small, inexpensive decision relative to stacks. Raising here would be thin and handcuffed (no blocker and weak showdown value); folding throws away >EV. Plan: call 250 to see the river, then fold to large river overbets when missed but call or extract value when I hit the straight (A/6) or when river pricing is correct.

I hold 2♠3♥ and the river is T♦-4♦-7♠-5♠-6♣ — I made the 6-high straight (2-3-4-5-6). Claude has been playing a wide limp/c-bet/call line so his river range contains many one-pair and marginal hands (T-x, small pairs, 5x, 4x, bluffs) that will pay a value bet. The only realistic hands that beat me are higher straights (a 3 or an 8), and I block some of those combos by holding the 3. With ~900 in the pot a value bet is correct; I choose roughly 2/3 pot (~600) to extract from worse hands while still keeping many callers. If he raises big I’ll re-evaluate, but betting for value now is the highest EV line.

On the river with J9d on a Td 4d 7s 5s 6c board, I have jack-high with no made hand. My semi-bluffing line (aggressive bets on flop and turn with a flush draw and overcard potential) didn't materialize into a completed hand. Minni's river raise to 600 after checking flop and turn is a significant tell - this line suggests either genuine strength (a pair that improved on the river, or a hand that checked back on the turn and is now confident) or a deliberate exploitation of my short-stack aggression. With only 10,150 chips remaining and facing a 600 call, I need positive expected value. Jack-high has minimal equity against Minni's likely holdings (pairs, better high cards). Even with pot odds of 2.5-1, I need approximately 28% equity to break even, and J-high likely has less than that against Minni's likely range. While occasional bluffs exist in heads-up play, this particular line (check-check-raise) is more consistent with genuine strength. I should fold and preserve chips for a better spot where I have superior equity or position. My short stack means I can't afford to make marginal calls that aren't clearly +EV.

Heads-up on the button with A♢7♡ is a strong, positionally advantaged hand. I want to take the initiative, isolate the big blind and fold out weaker hands while building a pot I can play postflop in position. A standard 3x-ish open (to 300 total) is the table norm and large enough to apply pressure without committing a large portion of my 29,800 stack — Claude has ~10,050 behind so this sizing keeps options open if he 3-bets. If he calls, I’ll play the flop in position; if he 3-bets I can re-evaluate based on sizing and my fold equity.

Raising to 900 total with pocket kings in heads-up play. KK is the second-best starting hand and demands aggressive play. Minni's button raise is standard, and a 3-bet to 900 applies appropriate pressure while building a pot I should win with this premium hand. This sizing is aggressive but not reckless given the stack sizes and current game state.

Heads-up on the button with A♦7♥ vs Claude's 3-bet to 900: this is a spot to use position and play postflop. Claude's 3-bet range heads-up will include a lot of Ax, broadways and pairs — A7o is often dominated by his value portion, so a 4-bet for value is thin and a small 4-bet (to 1500) won't fold enough hands while needlessly bloating the pot. Calling keeps the pot manageable, preserves stack depth and lets me use position to realize equity and apply pressure on favorable flops. Current pot is 1,200 and I need to add 600 to call; after calling effective stacks behind will be ~8,350 and SPR ≈ 4.6, which is playable. Plan: call to 900 total, evaluate the flop — on A‑x flops proceed cautiously vs heavy action, on dry/low flops look to c-bet for fold equity and on good draw/paired textures adjust range accordingly. If Claude 5-bets shove I will fold most of the time (we're dominated), but will continue postflop in position against standard 4-bet/call lines.

Betting 1100 with pocket kings on a dry Qh 2d 4c flop. This is a standard continuation bet (~60% of the 1800 pot) after my pre-flop 3-bet. KK is a premium hand that's likely ahead of Minni's calling range. The bet builds value, applies pressure, and gathers information about her holding. Stack management is healthy with 8150 remaining after this bet.

Heads-up on the button with A♦7♥ vs Claude’s preflop 3-bet — Q♥2♦4♣ is a very dry flop. Claude’s small ~1.1k stab into a 2.9k pot is a standard continuation size that will include both value (Qx, pocket pairs, some Ax) and a decent number of bluffs. I have Ace-high with an Ace blocker to a lot of his Ax combos and decent showdown value. Pot odds to call are ~1,100 to win ~4,000 (≈27.5% equity required), which is reasonable against a 3-bet/c-bet range. Raising here would be a thin semi-bluff with limited fold equity versus a 3-bettor and would bloat the pot with a marginal hand; folding is too passive given the board texture and odds. I will call 1,100 to use position and see a turn, then re-evaluate — if I pair the Ace I can extract value, if the turn blanks and he shows weakness I can take the pot, and if he barrels big with heavy action I can fold to preserve my big stack.

Betting 2200 on the turn with pocket kings. The board (Qh 2d 4c 6h) is relatively dry and the 6h doesn't complete dangerous draws or obviously improve Minni's likely calling range from the flop (which included QQ, AQ, AK, and underpairs). KK is still the strongest hand in most scenarios. This bet continues my aggressive narrative from the pre-flop 3-bet, builds value, and applies appropriate pressure in heads-up play. The sizing (~55% of the 4000 pot) is standard for value extraction while leaving me with 5950 chips remaining - sufficient stack for river decisions. This is consistent with optimal GTO play in heads-up situations with premium hands.

Heads‑up vs Claude (a preflop 3‑better) on a very dry runout Q♥‑2♦‑4♣‑6♥. I hold A♦7♥ — Ace‑high with one heart blocker but no made pair and effectively no legitimate draw. Claude’s line (3‑bet pre, c‑bet flop, another sizable turn bet) is consistent with a fairly value‑heavy range (Qx, medium/overpairs, some Ax/AK) plus a smaller portion of bluffs. Pot = 6,200 and I must call 2,200 to see the river; calling would make the pot ~8,400 so I need ≈26% equity to break even.

Raising is unattractive: I have little fold equity vs a 3‑bettor and would just bloat the pot or induce a shove from a shorter stacked opponent. Folding is too passive given the pot odds and the chance Claude is barreling some bluffs or missed broadway draws. Calling is the best balance — I keep the pot controlled, use position on the river, and retain the option to fold to heavy river aggression from Claude (he’ll be short‑stacked behind after this street). If I pair the Ace on the river I can extract value; if Claude checks I can take advantage of his hesitation; if he shoves big on the river I will fold unless I pair the Ace or the river is very favorable.

Decision: call 2,200 to see the river and re‑evaluate there (fold to large shove, continue vs small/medium action depending on the river).

River decision with pocket kings on a completed board (Qh 2d 4c 6h 8h). The 8h is a particularly dangerous river card—it completes flush draws (any Ah combination) and doesn't improve my hand. After Minni called three streets of aggression (pre-flop 3-bet, flop bet, turn bet), her range likely includes: strong hands like QQ, AA (which beat me), made flushes, or possibly broadway cards/underpairs. Given the action pattern and this problematic river card, I'm in a vulnerable spot with KK. With 5,950 chips remaining and an 8,400 pot, betting would be nearly pot-committing. Checking allows me to: (1) win the pot uncontested if she checks behind, (2) control losses if she bets with a stronger hand, and (3) avoid inflating the pot when my hand is likely either well-ahead or well-behind with little in-between. In heads-up play, this is a prudent line that respects the information from the river card while preserving my ability to make a fold decision if she shows strength.

Heads‑up on a very dry/hearty river with A♦7♥: Claude was the preflop 3‑better and barreled flop and turn, which is a relatively value‑heavy line (Qx, medium/overpairs, some Ax and occasional bluffs). The river brings a third heart (Q♥–2♦–4♣–6♥–8♥) so many made hands in his range are still beating my lone Ace; I only beat pure air/bluffs and maybe some tiny holdings that checked down. Pot = 8,400 and Claude has ~5,950 behind — a bet here would be a pure bluff attempt with limited fold equity versus a short‑stacked, previously aggressive 3‑better and would either bloat the pot when called or fold out only the weakest hands. Given my lack of a pair or a completed draw, the low likelihood of folding out his value range and the desire to avoid committing chips with a marginal hand, the lowest variance / highest EV play is to check back and take showdown. If Claude had bet I would re‑evaluate (call small bets cautiously given pot odds, fold to a large shove).

Heads-up play on the button with Ah-6h. This is a strong position with a premium hand (Ace with a suited kicker and backdoor flush draw). Minni has been aggressive throughout the tournament. With only 50 chips to call and excellent pot odds, raising to 200 is the correct play to:

1. Take control from the button - heads-up position is powerful
2. Gain fold equity and apply pressure
3. Define my hand's strength while maintaining flexibility
4. Extract value from position with a hand that plays well in position

The min-raise to 200 puts Minni in a difficult spot while risking a reasonable amount given the 14,300 stack size. This is standard button strategy in heads-up play.

Heads-up in the big blind with QcJh (QJ offsuit). Claude (button/SB) opened to 200; pot is 300 and I need 100 more to call (total commit 200). QJ is strong enough to defend here — facing a single opponent’s standard open, calling gets good pot odds (100 to win ~300) and lets me realize equity without bloating the pot out of position. I don’t want to 3-bet with a relatively non-polarized hand and then play a big pot OOP. Plan: call to 200, see the flop; if I pair or pick up a strong draw I can play aggressively, otherwise proceed cautiously and fold to heavy action when clearly behind.

Heads-up on a 9♦ 9♣ K♦ flop with Q♣ J♥. I have no pair and no meaningful draw (no diamonds, only two overcards with the K blocking a lot of my outs' utility). Claude’s opening range contains many Kx and pocket 9s that already beat me, and a bet here is unlikely to fold out enough better hands to justify bloating the pot OOP. Pot is small (400) and stacks are deep enough to wait for a cleaner spot. I’ll check to control the pot and realize equity; if I pair (Q or J) or pick up a strong read on a later street I can play more aggressively, otherwise I’ll fold to heavy action.

Minni checked on the flop after calling my pre-flop raise. The board is 9d-9c-Kd, a dry, paired texture. I have Ace-high with a backdoor flush draw. Minni's check suggests she likely missed this flop or has a marginal hand - a paired board with a King makes it difficult for her to have hit. I should bet here to: (1) apply pressure and win the pot outright since she demonstrated weakness by checking, (2) extract value if she has a weaker Ace or a missed draw, and (3) maintain my positional advantage. A bet of 250 (about 60% of pot) is appropriately sized for my position and stack size. If she folds, I win the pot; if she calls, I maintain flexibility on later streets with my Ace-high and backdoor flush draw. This is standard aggressive button play against a checking opponent on a connected but difficult board to hit.

I checked the flop as planned. On 9♦ 9♣ K♦ I have no pair, no diamond draw, only QJ with 6 outs to pair by the river (~24% equity). The price to continue (250 into a 650 pot) requires ~27.8% equity, so a call is slightly -EV. A raise as a bluff is unattractive — I don’t block K or 9 combos and I’ll be out of position in a bloated pot. With deep stacks I’ll conserve chips and wait for a clearer spot. Fold.

Heads-up on the button with T5o — open-raising is the +EV play. I’ll take the initiative and raise to 2BB (200 total) rather than limp/call: it uses my positional advantage, gets folds from weaker big-blind defenses, and keeps the pot manageable with a marginal but playable hand. If Claude 3-bets large I’ll fold (T5o plays poorly vs a 3-bet range); if he calls I’ll c-bet favorable flops and control the pot on uncoordinated/ace-high boards, using position to make better decisions postflop.

Calling 100 to win 300 provides 3:1 pot odds. In heads-up play, K4o is playable, especially with favorable pot odds and position on the flop. Minni's button raise range is wide, so I'm not necessarily behind. The call allows me to evaluate the flop with reasonable equity and decision-making opportunities post-flop.

With K4o on a paired Jd 3c 3d flop in heads-up play, I have merely King-high with a weak kicker. Against Minni's wide range, I'm likely behind most holdings. Checking allows me to keep the pot small with this marginal holding, gather turn information, and avoid overcommitting. The flopped pair on the board doesn't connect with my hand, and my equity is minimal.

I opened preflop to take the initiative with T5o and Claude just called. Flop J♦ 3♣ 3♦ is a pretty dry, low, double‑paired board where my range as the preflop aggressor contains a lot of overcards and bluffing combos. Claude checked — showing some weakness. I have almost no showdown value but decent fold equity here, so a small continuation bet is the +EV play: it folds many unpaired hands and keeps the pot manageable versus calling ranges. I’ll bet ~50% pot (200). Plan: if Claude folds I win the pot; if he calls I’ll pot‑control on the turn (check back most blanks and only continue with a realized pair or a very favorable card); if he raises I’ll fold — my hand has minimal equity versus a raise.

K4o (King-high with a weak four kicker) is too marginal to call on this paired board (Jd 3c 3d). While the pot odds are favorable at 3:1, my hand equity against Minni's wide button-raising range is likely only 20-30%, which falls short of the 33% threshold needed. Minni's bet indicates she has something, and my hand has no draw or hidden strength. With a modest chip stack relative to Minni, preserving chips with weak hands is crucial. Folding keeps me in position to accumulate chips with stronger holdings.

Heads-up play with T2o on the button. While position is favorable, T2o is a weak hand that doesn't warrant investment when I'm significantly short-stacked (14,300 vs 25,550). I'm down about 10,500 chips and need to pick spots carefully to rebuild. Folding weak hands pre-flop and waiting for playable holdings is crucial when short-stacked. I'll be more selective with my opening range and look for better spots (pairs, broadway cards, good wheel components) to make my stand. Minni is aggressive but I need hands that can win significant pots, not marginal holdings that put me in difficult post-flop situations.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind) with A♣4♦ — a hand that plays very well in heads-up pots. With 25650 chips (deep) and only Claude in the big blind (14200), I want to seize the initiative and steal the blind or build a pot with an A‑high advantage. The pot is 150 and I can raise to 200 (2x BB) — a standard, economical heads‑up open that puts pressure on a wide BB defending range while keeping the pot manageable postflop. If Claude 3-bets large I can fold and preserve my deep stack; if he calls I can play aggressively on favorable flops. I raise to 200 total.

Heads-up situation with a reasonable broadway hand (QT). Minni raised to 200 from the small blind, and I'm in the big blind needing to call 100 more. The pot odds are favorable (3:1 to call 100 to win 300), and QT is a perfectly reasonable hand to play in heads-up poker from the big blind. This hand can make straights, top pair, and has decent equity against Minni's wide raising range. The postflop position advantage as the big blind in heads-up is also valuable. Call and proceed to see the flop.

Checking the flop with QT on Jd 9h 6s in a heads-up spot. With an open-ended straight draw and decent high-card value, I want to keep the pot manageable given my shorter stack (14100 vs 25500). A check allows me to control variance, see another card, and respond to Minni's turn action rather than committing chips with a marginal hand. This balanced approach avoids getting out of line while keeping my options open.

Heads-up on a dry J♦9♥6♠ flop after my preflop open; Claude checked which often indicates a wide range of weak pairs, J/9 holdings, air, or slow-played pocket pairs. I have A♣4♦ — ace-high with a blocker to some A-high combos but no real showdown strength. A medium continuation bet ≈50% pot (200 into 400) puts pressure on his wide check/call range, folds out unpaired hands, and keeps the pot manageable if called. Plan: take the pot now if he folds; if he calls I'll re-evaluate the turn and only continue with strong equity or good board texture — fold to a sizable check-raise since my hand has limited value.

Calling Minni's flop bet with QT on Jd9h6s. I have an open-ended straight draw (8 outs for K/8) plus high card value, giving me approximately 35-40% equity. Pot odds are 4:1, requiring only 20% equity to call profitably. This is well above the break-even threshold. While I'm shorter stacked, this specific hand has excellent equity and multiple ways to improve on the turn. A check-call line is appropriate to see the next card while controlling variance with my shorter stack.

Turn bet with OESD (8 outs) against a checking opponent. My Queen-Ten has an open-ended straight draw on a Jd-9h-6s-3d board. With only 8 outs, I need to be aggressive with this equity while Minni has shown weakness by checking. A 400 chip bet into the 700 pot is sized appropriately for my stack (13,900) and creates immediate fold equity while building value when I hit. This is a spot where initiative and equity alignment favor aggression over a passive check-through approach.

Claude check-called my flop bet and now leads small (400 into a 1200 pot). I have A♣4♦ — no pair and only a few outs to improve, but the price is good: calling 400 to win 1600 (~25% break-even). His line can include value (Jx/9x/small pairs) and floats/bluffs; versus that mixed range my Ace‑high has roughly breakeven/plus equity. A raise would be a thin bluff against a player who has shown willingness to call; folding here would be too passive given the odds. I’ll call the 400 and re-evaluate on the river (fold to a large bet unless I pair or the river texture favors a bluff).

Missed my OESD with queen-high on the river. After semi-bluffing the turn and getting called, the Ah didn't improve my hand. Minni's river check suggests weakness or a marginal holding. Betting queen-high is neither good for value nor credible as a bluff. Checking back to showdown is the optimal play—I have reasonable showdown equity against most hands in her check range, and I'm managing my shorter stack (13,500 vs 24,900) appropriately by avoiding marginal betting situations.

River: J♦ 9♥ 6♠ 3♦ A♥. I have A♣4♦ — top pair, weak kicker. Heads‑up top pair is a strong value hand here. Claude’s line (check-call flop, check‑raise on the turn, then check the river) shows he had some initiative earlier but is showing a degree of caution now; his river check will include lots of Jx/9x/small pairs and missed draws that will call a value bet. Checking invites a free showdown or gives him a cheap showdown with hands that would call a bet; betting extracts thin value. I’ll bet for value ~2/3 pot to get called by worse hands (Jx/9x/medium pairs/missed draws). If he raises big I’ll re‑evaluate, but as played a standard value bet is best.

Folding queen-high on the river. I semi-bluffed the turn with an OESD, missed on the ace, and now face a river bet. While pot odds are 3.6:1 requiring only ~22% equity, my queen-high likely has 20-22% equity at best against Minni's range of value hands (aces, pairs, straights) and occasional bluffs. The turn check followed by river aggression is a pattern suggesting she has something. With 13,500 chips remaining and 59 hands left, I can afford to fold marginal showdown situations and wait for better spots with more confident holdings.

Heads-up from the small blind with QT-offsuit. This is a solid broadway hand that plays well in heads-up situations. Looking at the tournament status - we're 92 of 150 hands deep, and in heads-up play, I need to be much more aggressive with a wider range than in multi-way pots. QT is above-average and should be raised rather than called. A standard raise to 3x the big blind (300 total) is appropriate here, applying pressure while not committing too much of my stack.

Heads-up vs a button open to 300. I’m in the big blind with 4♣6♥ — a weak hand but facing a small price: calling 200 into a 400 pot gives ~33% break-even equity. Against a wide heads-up opening this hand has roughly that equity and some backdoor potential. I don’t want to 3‑bet light out of position and bloat the pot with nothing, nor fold away too many hands in heads-up play. I’ll call to see a flop and proceed cautiously: fold to strong aggression on dry boards, continue with any pair or decent draw, consider pot‑building only when I pick up real equity.

Out of position with 4♣6♥ on J♠7♥2♥ — no pair, only weak backdoor hearts/straight possibilities. Claude opened wide heads‑up, so he will c‑bet a lot; I don’t want to bloat the pot with junk or get raised off later streets. Check to control the pot, induce a c‑bet, and fold to meaningful pressure. I’ll only continue if I pair, pick up a strong draw, or face a very small probe bet worth a call. Preserves stack and lets me pick better spots post‑turn.

I'm playing heads-up from the button with QT of clubs. I raised pre-flop to 300 and Minni called, now checking the flop (7h-2h-Js). Minni's check indicates weakness - they likely missed the flop or have a medium-strength hand. In heads-up play, I need to be aggressive with broadway hands and apply pressure when my opponent shows weakness. QT has strong overcards (both Q and T beat most of Minni's likely holdings like lower pairs or AK/AQ type hands). The flop is relatively dry with few strong hands possible. I should bet to win the pot now or build a pot where my overcards have strong equity. A bet of 400 chips (roughly 2/3 pot) applies appropriate pressure while maintaining stack control.

I checked the flop with 4♣6♥ and Claude c‑bet 400 into a 1000 pot. That call would require ~28.6% equity (400/(1000+400)). My hand has no pair and only very weak backdoor outs (one heart for a runner‑runner flush, a few cards to pair). Immediate turn outs (three 4s + three 6s = 6 outs) give only ~12.8% to pair the turn and my overall river equity versus a reasonable heads‑up c‑betting range is below break‑even. I’m out of position and don’t want to inflate the pot with marginal, easily dominated holdings. With a big effective stack advantage I can wait for better spots to apply pressure. Fold and preserve chips.

Heads-up on the button with A♠K♣ (Ah Kc) and deep stacks — this is a clear open-raise spot. Calling 50 would give Claude good odds and keep too many speculative hands in; the min-raise (200) is fine but a slightly larger 3x BB sizing pressures weaker holdings and builds a pot I can play in position. I’ll raise to 300 total to extract value, gain fold equity, and keep postflop SPR reasonable. If Claude 3-bets I’ll reassess based on size and his tendencies.

5-3 offsuit is the worst starting hand in poker and I should fold it here. I'm in the Big Blind facing a raise from Minni (Button/SB). While Minni has been aggressive throughout the session, defending with such a poor hand out of position would be a leak. I have 13,700 in chips and need to rebuild my stack, but that requires playing strong hands in favorable spots, not bleeding chips with marginal holdings. This is a clear fold - one of the few hands where you should never defend.

4-10 offsuit is a weak, unconnected hand with poor kicker value. While I'm on the button and only need to call 50 more chips, the hand quality doesn't justify the call. Even with the favorable pot odds (2:1), this hand is below my threshold for head-up play. At hand 94 of 150, I should be selective and preserve chips for better opportunities. Minni has been fairly active, and I don't want to bloat the pot with marginal holdings out of position post-flop. Folding is the correct decision here.

Q♦T♣ is a strong heads-up opening hand and I want to take the initiative from the button. With deep stacks (26300 vs 13550) and post‑flop positional advantage, I’ll open‑raise to a standard 3x BB to build a pot and gain fold equity. I avoid the minimum to 200 because a 300 raise gets more respect and reduces cheap multiway/see‑the‑flop situations while still keeping risk manageable. If Claude folds I scoop the 150. If he calls I’ll play postflop in position—c‑betting favorable textures and controlling pot size on coordinated boards. If he 3‑bets large I’ll generally fold QTo; may consider calling very small 3‑bets given the deep stacks but will re‑evaluate then.

Folding 3-6o from the big blind against Minni's button raise. This is one of the worst hands in poker and I'm out of position. While I'm getting reasonable pot odds (need to call 200 into a 400 pot for 3:1), my hand has very poor equity and I have no positional advantage to compensate. Minni has been playing aggressively throughout the session, and 3-6o is far too weak to defend my blind against her raise. Folding preserves my stack for better opportunities.

T8h from the button in heads-up play is a solid opening hand with both straight and flush potential. With blinds at 50/100, I have position and can take the initiative. Raising to 200 represents a 2x raise of the big blind, which is a standard aggression level in heads-up poker. This accomplishes several things: (1) it gives me the lead in the hand, (2) it could take down the pot immediately, (3) if called, I maintain positional advantage postflop. My stack of 13,500 is moderate - I need to accumulate chips but also avoid getting unnecessarily short, so this sizing is appropriate. Given that Minni is aggressive (has been raising frequently throughout the session), I need to play back and use position effectively.

Heads-up vs Claude who opened to 200 from the button. I hold A♦Q♥ — a top-tier heads-up hand that plays well both preflop and postflop. I want to isolate and build value while denying cheap flops to speculative holdings, so I 3-bet. I choose 900 as a size that gets value from worse aces/ broadways and picks up fold equity, but doesn’t overcommit given deep stacks and OOP postflop. Postflop plan: continue as the aggressor with c-bets on dry/favorable textures, control pot size on very wet boards, and re-evaluate if Claude 4-bets (fold to extremely polarizing or shove-heavy 4-bets, call smaller 4-bets to play postflop).

T8h (ten-eight of hearts) is a solid speculative hand in heads-up play with both straight and flush draw potential. Analysis: (1) Minni's 3-bet to 900 shows strength or aggression, but in heads-up poker his re-raising range is naturally wide; (2) Pot odds: I'm getting 1100:700 (1.57:1 to call), which is reasonable; (3) Hand equity: T8h has approximately 40-45% equity against a typical 3-bet range, with multiple ways to improve (8 straight outs, flush outs, pair outs); (4) Stack management: Calling 700 more maintains healthy stack depth (remaining ~12,650) while keeping me in position for post-flop play where I can exercise positional advantage on the flop; (5) Re-raising would overrepresent hand strength with a marginal hand, potentially leading to larger commitments with a hand that plays better in position on later streets; (6) Folding is too passive given hand quality and odds. The call allows me to see the flop with good equity and play my position strength post-flop.

I 3‑bet preflop with A♦Q♥ and Claude flat‑called. The flop 9h‑6s‑9s is a bad texture for my hand: it pairs the board and heavily favors Claude’s flatting range (pocket pairs, 9x, TT+/overpairs). I’m out of position and currently have only ace‑high — any single c‑bet here would be a bluff with limited fold equity because many of his call hands will continue. With ~12.6k effective stacks and a pot of 1.8k I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP with marginal equity.

Plan: check for pot control and to realize equity cheaply. If Claude fires a small bet I’ll call to see a turn (my A/Q can improve), but I’ll be prepared to fold to a sizable raise or large bet that strongly indicates a set/overpair. If he checks back I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn and only build the pot if I pair an Ace/Queen or get a favorable runout.

With T♥8♥ on 9♥6♠9♠, I have a strong draw: 9 hearts for a flush, 3 sevens for a straight, plus pair outs (tens and eights). That's 18+ outs giving me approximately 35-40% equity. Minni's flop check after 3-betting preflop suggests weakness or slow-play. I maintain positional advantage and initiative from my preflop raise, so continuing with aggression is the natural play. Betting 850 (roughly half the pot) applies appropriate pressure, builds value when I have equity, and keeps my stack in healthy shape with two more streets remaining. This bet size is balanced and consistent with exploiting Minni's passive flop action.

I 3‑bet preflop with A♦Q♥ and checked the flop for pot control. The 9♥‑6♠‑9♠ texture heavily favors Claude’s flatting range (pocket pairs, 9x, some overpairs) and my hand is currently only ace‑high. Claude’s bet 850 into the ~1800 pot is a modest c‑bet; calling 850 into a 2650 pot requires ≈24% equity, which is reasonable to try to realize equity and see a turn. Raising OOP with no pair would bloat the pot and is a poor bluff here because many hands that call my 3‑bet will continue. I’ll call 850 to keep the pot controlled, see a turn, and re‑evaluate: if I pair an A/Q I can build for value; if Claude fires a large turn shove or a very strong sizing I’ll fold to that line.

I 3‑bet preflop with A♦Q♥ and called Claude’s small flop C‑bet on 9♥‑6♠‑9♠ to keep the pot under control — that texture heavily favors his calling range (pocket pairs, 9x, some overpairs). The J♠ on the turn doesn’t help my hand (I still only have ace‑high) and actually brings a third spade, increasing the chance of completed two‑spade flushes in his range. Betting OOP here as a bluff has very thin fold equity and would bloat a pot I’m likely behind in; raising is even worse. I will check to control the pot, realize equity, and force Claude to show strength; plan is to call small turn bets to see a river but fold to large/shove sizing, and only build the pot myself if I pair the A or Q (or pick up a clear read to exploit).

Turn continuation with strong draw. With T♥8♥ on 9♥6♠9♠J♠, I have approximately 18+ outs including multiple straight draws (Q, K, 7), flush draw (any heart), and pair outs. Minni's turn check after 3-betting preflop and calling my flop bet suggests weakness or a marginal holding. Betting 2,000 accomplishes several objectives: (1) wins the pot immediately if Minni folds, (2) builds the pot with 35-40% equity, (3) maintains aggressive posture consistent with my preflop raise and flop bet, (4) manages stack effectively (remaining ~9,800 if called). The Jack on the turn completes more straight possibilities, supporting an aggressive line. Minni's passive action provides good fold equity.

I have A♦Q♥, out of position, pot is 5,500 and Claude has put in a 2,000 turn bet. Calling 2,000 to see the river makes the final pot 7,500, so I need ≈26.7% equity to justify a call. Claude flat‑called my 3‑bet pre and c‑bet a modest 850 on the paired 9‑9‑6 flop, which indicates a range weighted to pocket pairs/overpairs and some broadway/spade combos — but also includes non‑pair broadways and two‑spade holdings that can be barreled. I only have 6 clean outs to pair an A or Q (~13% on the river), but I also have showdown value versus unpaired broadways and I get good pot odds to continue cheaply one more card. Raising OOP as a bluff here would bloat the pot and is thin on this texture, and folding now would be too passive given the price and remaining stacks. Plan: call 2,000 to realize equity and re‑evaluate on the river — fold to a large shove/clearly polarizing raise, but proceed to value or call small river bets if I pair up or if Claude shows weakness.

Board runs out 9h-6s-9s-Js-Kh and I’m left with only A♦Q♥ (ace-high). Claude’s line — calling my 3-bet, a modest flop c-bet and a sizable turn barrel — is very value-weighted (pocket 9s, other pocket pairs/overpairs, Jx that improved, or completed spade flushes). Betting as a bluff here has very little fold equity against a caller of flop and turn and would bloat a pot I’m likely behind in. Checking preserves showdown, lets me catch any missed spade/bluff attempts, and avoids putting more chips at risk vs his value-heavy range. If Claude bets a small/medium river I can call for value/bluff-catch; if he jams an over-committal shove I should fold. Decision: check and go to showdown.

River brick card (K♥) completes the board. With T♥8♥, my actual holding is a pair of nines (9♥9♠) with K-J-T kickers - a decent showdown hand but not strong enough to confidently bet for value. Minni's river check after calling two streets of aggression suggests a marginal holding or weak hand. Key considerations: (1) My hand has legitimate showdown value as a pair, beating lower pairs and weak ace-high holdings; (2) The river K doesn't help complete my draws, so my value betting range is limited; (3) Betting here risks inducing folds from weaker hands or getting raised/called by stronger holdings; (4) My stack position (9800) is reasonable - I don't need to take marginal risks; (5) Minni's passive line (check-call-call-check) suggests strength/value play isn't working; (6) Taking the showdown with a pair has reasonable equity against Minni's likely holdings (missed draws, weaker pairs, weak aces). Checking allows me to go to showdown efficiently with a hand that plays better defensively than as a bluff.

Heads-up on the button with Jc7h — a marginal hand, but very playable in position. Pot is 150 and I only have 50 to call, but I prefer to take the initiative and raise to 2x the big blind to build the pot with position and apply pressure to Claude’s wide BB defending range. Raising to 200 total (150 more) gets folds from many weak hands, preserves fold equity, and lets me play postflop last. If he 3-bets big I’ll fold most of the time; if he calls I’ll c-bet or double-barrel selectively based on board texture.

In heads-up play against a small raise from the Small Blind, I should defend my Big Blind with reasonable equity hands. Ad5s has showdown value with the ace, and I'm getting 3:1 pot odds. The pot is only 300 with 100 to call, and Minni's small raise from SB suggests a typical steal attempt. I can exploit position and board texture post-flop with this hand. Calling is superior to folding here given the pot odds, stack sizes, and heads-up dynamics.

Minni checked the flop, indicating weakness. With Ad5s on a three-heart board (4h8hKh), I have an extremely powerful hand: the nut flush draw (9 outs) plus ace-high. On a board with three unpaired hearts, Minni's check from a small pre-flop raise is a clear sign of a missed steal attempt. I have multiple ways to win here: (1) win the pot immediately with a bet if Minni folds, (2) get value from weaker hands if they call, or (3) improve to the flush with strong equity. A bet of 250 (about 60% of pot) is appropriate for building the pot with my draw while keeping Minni's remaining stacks in play. This exploits their weakness while maintaining good pot odds and position control.

Claude led 250 into a ~400-chip pot on a very wet heart flop (4h 8h Kh). I hold Jc7h — no pair but one heart, which gives me 4 hearts with the board (9 hearts left). My chance to hit a heart by the river is ~35%, and calling 250 into a 650 pot requires ~27.8% equity (250/(650+250)=27.8%), so the pot odds justify a call. Claude’s flop bet is consistent with Kx, single-heart holdings, or some bluffs; a raise to 500 would often be called by Kx or two-heart combos and unnecessarily inflates the pot out of position if I miss. With a deep effective stack I prefer to use position and see the turn — I can bet or shove for value if I complete the flush or improve, or fold to heavy action on a blank turn. Call 250 to realize my equity and keep the weaker parts of his range in.

Minni checked the turn with the deuce hitting, indicating weakness. With the nut flush draw (9 outs), I have both strong draw equity and fold equity. Betting 500 applies pressure on her marginal holdings while building the pot with my significant equity. This semi-bluff leverages my strong draw and position to win the pot either immediately or when my flush completes on the river.

Claude has shown strength by leading the very wet heart flop and continuing on the turn (500 into a 900 pot, pot now 1400). Calling 500 into the 1400 pot means I need ≈26.3% equity (500 / (1400+500)). I hold Jc7h: I have 9 heart outs to make a flush on the river (9/46 ≈ 19.6%) plus 2 non-heart Js and 3 non-heart 7s that give me additional distinct pair-outs (total unique outs ≈ 14 → 14/46 ≈ 30.4% naive). That raw equity comfortably covers the pot odds. Claude’s line is consistent with many Kx value hands and some made-heart combos; some of my outs (non-heart pairs) will still lose to Kx, and some flush outs could be dominated if Claude already holds a higher heart, but on balance calling is +EV. I’m in position — I can see the river and use the information to extract value if I hit or to fold/control losses if I miss. Raising now as a semi-bluff has limited fold equity vs a Kx-heavy range and unnecessarily bloats the pot with only one card to come. Plan: call the 500, evaluate the river — if I complete the flush I’ll extract value; if I miss I’ll fold to heavy action unless the river card or showdown odds justify a call.

River value bet with top pair (pair of aces with K-5 kickers) after Minni checked, indicating weakness. The Ah completing the four-flush doesn't help her draw likely, and my pair of aces is the strongest hand in most scenarios. A 900 bet extracts value from weaker pairs and draws that missed while maintaining fold equity. Stack sizes are favorable for this bet size, and her passive line strongly suggests a checkdown/fold with inferior hands.

I hit a heart flush on the river (board: 4h 8h Kh 2c Ah; my Jc7h → A‑high heart flush using 7h). Claude’s line — calling preflop, c‑bet flop 250, betting turn 500 and river 900 — is consistent with Kx/medium pairs and with single‑heart draws; to beat my flush he must hold a heart higher than 7 (9h/T h/Jh/Qh) or a two‑heart combo. Pot is 2800 and his river bet is 900, so I need roughly 900/(2800+900) ≈ 24.3% equity to justify a call. A quick combinatoric estimate for him holding one of the four higher hearts (conditional on the seen river) is on the order of ~15–18%, below my breakeven. Many hands in his continuing range (Kx, smaller pairs, bluffs) I beat. Raising has limited upside — it will fold out lots of worse hands, can get re‑raised by the few hands that beat me, and bloats the pot unnecessarily — so the lowest‑variance +EV play is to call and take the showdown. I call 900.

2-4 is the weakest hand in poker with minimal equity even in heads-up play. While I'm in position with a short stack requiring opportunistic aggression, this hand doesn't warrant investing chips. The hand has poor connectivity, no high card value, and limited drawing potential. Even to win the 150 chips in the pot at 3:1 odds, my win rate with 2-4 against Minni's random big blind range doesn't justify a call. Better to fold and preserve my 7,900-chip stack for stronger holdings where I can apply more effective pressure.

Heads-up on the button I should be opening very wide. Q2o is marginal but perfectly reasonable to raise in heads-up to seize initiative and pressure the BB. Pot is small (150) and the minimum raise to 200 is a cheap, standard sizing that keeps the pot manageable while maximizing fold equity against Claude’s 7,800 stack. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet, call or proceed postflop with position if Claude just calls, c-bet favorable textures and control the pot on dry boards. This play maximizes expected value with minimal risk.

In heads-up play, defending the big blind with moderate pot odds is standard. 9s-5s has playability with straight and flush possibilities. Minni's small raise to 200 suggests a wider range. With short-stack dynamics, I should see the flop and evaluate based on board texture. Calling 100 to win 300 is reasonable given the game situation.

Semi-bluff with 9s5s on Jc8h6h - I have two main draws (gutshot straight with 4 outs, four-flush with 9 outs) providing approximately 30%+ equity. Betting 200 (50% of pot) is appropriate sizing to apply pressure and take down the pot while I have fold equity and solid equity value. Stack dynamics (7700 vs 31900) suggest aggressive play with draws is warranted.

Claude has bet 200 into ~600 (a ~1/3-pot bet), giving me about 4:1 pot odds (I need ≈20% equity to call). I opened very wide preflop in heads-up and I have position — Qs2c has little showdown value (no pair, no hearts) but against Claude’s continuation/float-heavy range I still have borderline equity and the ability to realize fold equity on later streets. Raising to 400 here would be a thin bluff that can be easily called by better hands and may commit me unnecessarily; folding relinquishes position and is too weak given the tiny price. I’ll call 200, keep the pot manageable, and re-evaluate on the turn: take value if I pair or shove/raise only with a clear plan; fold to significant aggression on a blank turn.

Short stack push with pair of nines on the turn. I've improved from drawing hand to made hand. With only 7200 chips remaining against a much deeper stack (31700), going all-in generates maximum fold equity while I have a legitimate hand that beats many bluffs. The pot is 800, so my bet is 9:1 odds, appropriate for my tournament position. This leverages the short-stack advantage in heads-up play where aggressive play with reasonable hands is crucial.

Fold. Claude’s turn shove is massive relative to the pot (pot ~8,000, I must call 7,200 -> total pot after a call ~15,200), so I need roughly 7,200/15,200 ≈ 47% equity to justify a call. I hold Q♠2♣ — no hearts, no pair — and the board is J♣ 8♥ 6♥ 9♥ (three hearts). My realistic outs (three remaining Queens + three remaining Twos ≈ 6 outs) give ~6/46 ≈ 13% chance to improve on the river, and many of those outs are dead or still lose to hands Claude is representing (two hearts in hand → flush, Jx/9x/two-pair). Claude’s line (small flop raise then huge turn shove) is highly polarized toward strong value or a very rare bluff. Calling (or min-raising) here is a large, negative-EV commitment of ~7.2k with far too little equity. Preserve the 31.7k stack and fold.

In heads-up play from the button with Q2o, calling the additional 50 chips is prudent. The pot odds (3:1) are acceptable, and I can see a flop cheaply. While Q2o is weak, winning uncontested pots and hitting queen-high are viable paths. Folding too frequently in heads-up will erode my chip stack. Raising with this hand commits too many chips given the weakness and my chip disadvantage. A conservative call preserves stack while keeping me in the hand.

Heads-up with A5 suited (Ad5d) is a strong, very playable hand — particularly against a wide button calling range. I have a large stack advantage so I should be aggressive and take the initiative preflop to build the pot and deny free equity to weaker hands. A standard 3x-sized raise to 300 is consistent with the table, gives value vs worse aces and many broadway hands, and preserves fold equity while not overcommitting. If Claude 3-bets I’ll re-evaluate (fold or call depending on sizing); if he calls I’ll C-bet favorable flops and play straightforwardly OOP. Raising to 300 maximizes my EV in this spot.

In heads-up play, I've already committed 50 chips to the pot as SB with Q2o. While this is a very weak hand, the pot odds of 2:1 justify the marginal call to see a flop. Against a likely wide BB raising range, Q2o has sufficient equity. Being short-stacked argues for discipline, but I should conserve chips by taking favorable spots to see cheap flops and compete. The sunk 50 chips shouldn't factor into the decision—only the marginal call of 200 more matters, and that's acceptable at these odds.

I was the preflop aggressor and the flop is Jc Qc 3c — a monotone, fairly wet board. I hold Ad5d: Ace-high with no club and no pair, so my hand has little showdown value or equity vs club draws and made Q/J hands. Heads‑up I should still c‑bet to use my initiative and fold out weaker Kx, small pairs and unpaired broadways while keeping the pot manageable. I’ll make a standard ~1/2‑pot continuation bet (300 into 600). If Claude raises big or jams, I’ll fold (I’m dominated or up against a flush); if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn and proceed only with improvement or favorable turn cards/textures.

Calling 300 with a pair of queens on a Jc 3c Qc board. Getting 3:1 pot odds (900 chips to call 300), I need approximately 25% equity to break even. My pair of queens likely has solid equity against Minni's c-bet range, especially on a two-flush board where many of their hands are draws or moderate holdings. As a severely short-stacked player (8000 chips vs 31100), I cannot afford to fold marginal hands with reasonable equity. I will call and re-evaluate on the turn.

I raised preflop and c‑bet the monotone Jc Qc 3c flop to use initiative, but Claude’s call on that board keeps a range that dominantly beats my Ace‑high: any club (nut/near‑nut flush), Qx/Jx, sets and medium pairs. The turn 7♦ is essentially a blank for my hand — I still only have Ace‑high (and even a diamond river would only make four diamonds, not a flush), so my equity vs his calling range is poor. With a 1.2k pot and huge stack advantage (I have 31.1k vs his 7.7k), pot control is correct: don’t barrel into a likely made hand and avoid committing chips with almost no showdown value. I’ll check and reevaluate on a river card — fold to significant aggression, call small bets for info or induce bluffs on a favourable river.

Minni checked the turn, indicating weakness or a draw. I hold a pair of queens (top pair), which is likely ahead of their range. As a severely short-stacked player, I need to maximize value when I likely have the best hand. A bet of 1500 chips builds the pot substantially without overcommitting, applying pressure to draws and marginal holdings while extracting value from the likely weaker part of Minni's range. The check suggests this is a good spot to bet for value.

I was the preflop aggressor and c-bet the monotone Jc-Qc-3c flop to fold out weaker hands. Claude’s call on the flop and now a 1500 bet into a 2700 pot on the turn is very strong here — his line is weighted toward hands that beat my Ace‑high: two-club combos (already a made flush), Qx/Jx, sets or strong pairs. The turn 7♦ is a blank for Ad5d — I have virtually no realistic way to improve to a hand that consistently wins (only an unlikely river A helps). Calling 1500 to win 4200 requires ~35.7% equity, which I don’t have vs his likely value-heavy range. Raising as a bluff (min‑raise to 3000) is too thin on a wet board with many made hands that won’t fold. I have a huge stack advantage and don’t need to gamble here — preserve chips and wait for a better spot. Fold.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with Js9s — a very playable, suited hand with good postflop equity and positional advantage. Against a single big blind (Claude, ~88bb) I want to take the initiative and apply pressure; Claude has shown a tendency to defend but isn’t an ultra‑aggro 3‑bettor. I’ll open‑raise to a standard 3xBB sizing (300 total) to get fold equity, build the pot when I’m likely ahead, and keep the SPR deep enough to maneuver postflop. If he folds I pick up the blinds; if he calls I have backdoor flush/straight potential and can continue with a range that includes many bluffs and strong hands. If he 3‑bets large I’ll re‑evaluate (fold or call depending on sizing and reads).

5-4 offsuit is one of the weakest hands in poker and is not worth defending from the big blind even against a standard raise. While I'm getting 3:1 odds (200 to call into 400 pot), this hand has poor win rates and will put me in difficult out-of-position spots post-flop against an aggressive opponent. With 8800 chips and needing to accumulate value-positive situations for the remaining 50 hands, folding weak hands and waiting for stronger spots is the correct long-term strategy.

Heads-up poker requires a significantly wider range on the Button. K-4 is a playable hand in this context, getting 3:1 odds with positional advantage. Standard call to see the flop and maintain reasonable aggression in short-handed play.

I’m in the BB with 5♦3♠ facing a limp from the button. This is a very weak, low-card offsuit hand and I’ll be out of position post‑flop, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with marginal holdings. A small preflop raise to try to steal is an option, but given the hand and OOP dynamics I’ll keep the pot small: check and see a flop. Plan: fold to a large c‑bet, but call small bets on favorable textures (any 3/5, paired board or good backdoor draws) and look for cheap realizations of equity or opportunities to bluff on later streets.

Board is T♣9♣6♣ and I hold 5♦3♠. I have no club, no pair and essentially no immediate equity — I’m dominated by any club and have only remote backdoor possibilities. Claude limped pre so his range can include many clubs, pairs and strong made hands. I’m out of position and don’t want to bloat the pot with a worthless hand. Best line is pot control: check and fold to meaningful aggression. I will only consider calling a very tiny probe (a token bet) to see a cheap turn, but will fold to pot-sized or larger C-bets. Action: check to keep the pot small and re-evaluate on the turn.

Checking K-4 on a three-club flop. My hand is marginal (king-high with weak kicker), and my only draw is a 4-high flush. Minni's check suggests weakness, so checking preserves chips and keeps the pot manageable. With a short stack, I should avoid bloating pots with marginal hands and wait for better opportunities.

Board T♣9♣6♣T♠ and I hold 5♦3♠ — no club, no pair, no real equity. Claude limp‑called pre and both checked the flop, so his range is wide but includes clubs, pocket pairs and tens. The turn pairing the ten significantly increases the value of hands I’m behind (top pair/trips, full houses with a ten, plus backdoor clubs). Pot is tiny (200) relative to stacks; there’s no reason to bloat it with a dead hand out of position. I’ll check and give up to any meaningful bet. I would only consider calling a token probe (very small, << pot) to see a cheap river, but will fold to pot‑sized or larger aggression. If Claude checks back I’ll re‑evaluate on the river but won’t voluntarily build the pot here.

Minni has checked on both flop and turn, indicating weakness. Currently I have K-high with a potential flush draw (4c gives me club holdings with three clubs on board). While K-4 is marginal, my opponent's passivity suggests my king may be ahead, or at minimum the pot can be won with aggression. In heads-up play at this stack depth (8700 vs 31100), I should exploit checking patterns and apply controlled pressure. A small bet of 100 chips (reasonable sizing relative to the 200-chip pot) accomplishes multiple objectives: (1) wins the pot immediately if opponent folds, (2) keeps pot manageable while gathering information, (3) maintains aggressive posture needed for short-stack survival. If called, I can reassess river action based on Minni's response pattern.

Board T♣9♣6♣T♠, my hand 5♦3♠ — no club, no pair, only six clean outs (three 5s + three 3s) → ~13% to improve on the river. Pot is 300 and Claude’s turn bet is 100; calling 100 would be for a 400 pot (need ~25% equity). My raw equity (~13%) is well below the pot‑odds threshold, and Claude’s line (checked flop then a small turn bet) is consistent with many made hands (club flushes, Tx, 9x, medium pairs) that I cannot beat. I already planned to give up to any meaningful turn aggression. Preserve chips and fold rather than calling a negative‑EV probe from out of position.

Heads-up on the button with A♦K♦ — a premium hand. Pot is 150 and I only need to call 50, but I should take the initiative and raise for value and fold equity. Standard sizing here is about 3x the BB; raising to 300 builds the pot, denies a cheap flop, and capitalizes on position. Claude has a ~8,800 stack so this sizing leaves plenty of room postflop and still allows me to respond to a 3-bet (call or 4-bet depending on sizing). I will not limp AKs — raise to 300.

95 offsuit is a weak hand with poor equity. As the big blind facing a raise, and with a short 8800 chip stack, calling 200 more is not justified. I should fold and wait for premium hands to make my short-stack stand. Preservation of chips is critical with 47 hands remaining in the tournament.

Heads-up play with 8-9 offsuit is a standard call. The cost is minimal (50 more), pot odds are favorable (3:1), and folding would bleed equity unnecessarily. Being short-stacked, I need to play hands with reasonable equity. 8-9 has good connectivity for straights and is well above the folding threshold in this situation.

Heads-up on the button (Claude) limped/called into my big blind. I hold J♦5♠ — a marginal, non‑suited hand and I’ll be out of position postflop (he’s on the button). I could raise to try to isolate, but with a wide limp range and Claude’s positional advantage I prefer pot control and to realize equity cheaply. Checking keeps the pot small, lets me call small continuation bets on favorable flops (top pair, backdoor possibilities) and fold to significant aggression on dry boards. With a big stack advantage I’ll pick better spots to apply pressure later.

Out of position with J♦5♠ on K♣ Q♦ 7♥. I have no pair and essentially no realistic draws (one diamond in hand + one on board doesn’t create a flush draw; a straight would need both T and 9). Claude limped a very wide range heads‑up, so he can have lots of Kx/Qx, small pairs, or air — but I’m still behind or drawing almost dead to most of that range. Betting OOP with a naked J5 here is losing EV: better to keep the pot small and avoid bloating the pot with a weak hand. I’ll check and either call a very small probe to see a cheap turn or fold to meaningful aggression. If he checks back I’ll re‑evaluate the turn and only start applying pressure if I pick up real equity (J/5 or a favorable runout).

Betting 150 on the flop with an open-ended straight draw. Minni's check indicates weakness, and I'm in position to take initiative. This is a semi-bluff that either wins the pot now or allows me to continue with reasonable equity. Short-stacked and heads-up, I need to play hands with potential aggressively rather than passively check through.

Heads‑up OOP with J♦5♠ on K♣ Q♦ 7♥. Claude limped a wide range preflop and now fires a small flop probe (150 into a 350 pot — I’m getting roughly 30% pot odds). I have no pair and basically no real draws, so raising OOP is a poor bluff and folding to such a small bet is too tight. I’ll call the 150 to keep the pot controlled, realize equity cheaply, and re‑evaluate on the turn — folding to significant turn aggression unless I improve (pair a J/5 or pick up a clear equity-changing card) or using my big stack to apply pressure only on better boards.

I improved to top pair on the turn (J♦5♠ on K♣ Q♦ 7♥ J♠). Claude limped/called pre and made a tiny flop probe (150 into the pot), which represents a very wide range — from air and small pairs to K/Q and occasional Jx. With a vulnerable kicker out of position I want to extract value and deny a free river card, but keep the pot controlled. Betting about half the pot (250 into the 500) prices worse Jx and other marginal hands to call while still allowing me to fold to severe aggression; if Claude check-raises or shoves big I’ll give it credit (he can easily have K/Q/sets) and fold rather than commit my deep stack with a weak kicker. Plan: bet 250, call small responses, re-evaluate on the river if called.

With approximately 17.4% equity (8 outs), I'm getting 4:1 pot odds but need 20% to break even. This is a marginal -EV fold. Minni's turn raise indicates a made hand, and my straight draw doesn't have sufficient equity against their likely holdings. In heads-up play with a chip deficit, preserving my stack for better opportunities is the prudent play.

Heads-up on the button with Q♦7♦ — marginal but definitely open-raiseable. I have postflop position and a deep stack advantage, so I want to take the initiative, deny the BB good odds to see cheap flops, and build pots when I hit. A standard 3x-ish raise to 300 is big enough to discourage very loose calls but small relative to my stack so I keep flexibility. Plan: if Claude just calls, play aggressively in position (c-bet good flops, check back bad ones); if he 3-bets large I’ll let this one go — Q7s isn’t worth a big preflop confrontation against an aggressive 3-bet.

In heads-up play against Minni's button raise to 300, I'm facing a call of 200 more to win 400, giving me 2:1 pot odds. K5s has reasonable equity against a wide button raising range - it can make top pair with the king, has flush draw potential, and some straight possibilities. With only two players remaining in the tournament, wider calling ranges are justified in the big blind. Minni has been playing aggressively throughout, so their button raise likely includes a wide range of hands. Calling is more prudent than 3-betting with this marginal holding, allowing me to play the hand well postflop with position advantage.

K5 is weak top pair with a marginal kicker on a dry 4♦9♣2♣ board. Minni's flop check suggests weakness, but I lack the hand strength to value bet profitably. Checking back preserves chips, allows a free turn card to improve (potential flush draw completion), and keeps the pot manageable. In heads-up play, controlling bet sizes with marginal holdings is critical to long-term success. I can reassess the turn with more information.

I opened preflop and Claude just flat-called. The flop (4♦ 9♣ 2♣) is very dry and misses a large portion of a caller’s range (broadways, many small pairs, suited gappers). I have Q♦7♦ — no pair, only a backdoor (runner-runner) diamond possibility — but I have position and the betting initiative. Claude checked, which looks like weakness or a hand he wants to see a free card with. Against a short-ish stack (Claude ~8,250) and with a big effective stack advantage, applying pressure is profitable. A half‑pot c-bet (~300 into 600) gives good fold equity vs unpaired hands and smaller pairs, keeps the pot manageable if called, and balances aggression.
