If he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn (barrel on favorable cards, check back on blank turns to realize equity); if he raises big or shoves, I’ll fold this unimproved Q7s. Betting 300 maximizes immediate fold equity while preserving flexibility postflop.

Facing a 300 flop bet on 4♦9♣2♣ with K♥5♥ (top pair, weak kicker). I have 2:1 pot odds (300 to win ~900), requiring approximately 33% equity to call profitably. In heads-up play against Minni's aggressive button/SB range, K5 has solid equity:

1. Top pair (king-high) likely ahead of many hands in Minni's c-betting range
2. Small flush draw to the 5 (3 additional outs)
3. Wheel draw potential (3 makes a wheel, 1 out)
4. Against Minni's wide range, estimated equity is 35-45%

With significant chip deficit (8250 vs 30850), I need to leverage decent equity spots with playable hands. Folding top pair with reasonable odds would be overly passive. Calling allows me to:
- See the turn card with more information
- Play the hand competitively in heads-up
- Fold if a scary overcard arrives and Minni continues

The dry flop (4-9-2) doesn't contain overcards that would crush K5, making this a straightforward call with positive expected value.

With K♥5♥ on 4♦9♣2♣T♦, I have top pair with 11 flush outs giving me solid equity against Minni's checked turn. In heads-up play with a severe chip deficit, I must leverage decent equity spots aggressively. Minni's turn check indicates potential weakness. Betting 700 (approx 58% of pot) applies pressure, seeks value from weaker holdings, and maintains aggressive gameplay with positive expected value given my equity and fold equity potential.

Board is 4♦‑9♣‑2♣‑T♦, I hold Q♦7♦. The turn gives me a big drawing hand: I have a four‑card diamond draw (9 diamonds left) plus two clean pair outs (three queens and three sevens) — about 15 distinct outs ≈ 32–33% to improve on the river. Claude’s turn bet of 700 into the 1900 pot makes the pot after his bet ~2600, so I’m being asked to call 700 to win 2600 (need ≈27% equity). My raw equity (~33%) > pot odds, so a call is +EV. Raising as a semi‑bluff would gain some fold equity but will often get called by hands that already beat me (T, 9, some pairs) and commit too many chips with only ~33% immediate equity. With the deep effective stacks I prefer to realize equity in position and keep the pot controlled. Plan: call 700; if I hit a diamond or pair on the river I’ll value bet or extract; if I miss I’ll evaluate river action and fold to large aggression.

River value bet with two pair (K5 on 4-9-2-T-5 board). Strong made hand in heads-up play. Minni's river check indicates weakness/showdown strength rather than a strong holding. Betting 1000 extracts value from worse hands (pairs, missed draws, lower two pairs) while remaining sizing-consistent with aggressive line (similar to turn bet). Two pair is the kind of hand where we want to build the pot against a checking opponent in heads-up play, especially given chip deficit position.

Board is 4♦-9♣-2♣-T♦-5♠ and I hold Q♦7♦ — I missed my backdoor diamond and didn’t pair. Claude bet 1,000 into a 2,600 pot (pot is 3,600 after his bet), so I must call 1,000 to win 3,600. Break‑even equity = 1,000 / (3,600 + 1,000) ≈ 21.7%. I have no showdown equity except vs bluffs, so this is a pure bluff‑catcher decision. Claude’s line (call pre, check/call flop, lead on turn for 700, small river bet 1,000) includes many thin value hands (9x, Tx, small pairs) but also missed clubs/other draws and some turn bluffs — a nontrivial bluff frequency. With my huge stack advantage calling a small amount to catch those bluffs is +EV; raising would commit too many chips with no value and will be called or shoved by hands that beat me. I’ll therefore call the 1,000 and fold at showdown only if he shows value.

Folding 8-7 offsuit from the button with a critical short stack. While heads-up play is wide, I'm down to only ~27% of the effective stack and need to preserve chips for premium hands where I can maximize equity. 8-7o is too weak to get involved with when short-stacked against an opponent with 3x my chips. Will wait for better opportunities.

Heads-up on the button with J♣ J♦ — a very strong, standard raising hand. With stacks 29,150 vs 10,700 and blinds 50/100, I want to build the pot and get value while keeping post‑flop options. I’ll open to 300 (≈3x the big blind) — a standard size that extracts value from worse Ax/Kx/pairs, keeps worse hands in, and preserves flexibility against a 3‑bet. If Claude 3‑bets moderately I’ll mostly call and play postflop (JJ is ahead of his likely 3‑betting range); if he 4‑bets shove I’ll evaluate but am inclined to call given my stack and hand equity. Postflop plan: c‑bet dry boards for value, pot‑control or check‑call on Ace/King heavy or highly coordinated boards, and size value bets where appropriate.

I'm holding pocket nines in the big blind facing a button raise to 300 from Minni. This is a clear call situation for several reasons:

1. Hand Strength: Pocket nines is a solid hand with decent equity against Minni's button raising range, which is likely quite wide given her aggressive play throughout the session.

2. Position & Pot Odds: I'm in the BB and need to call only 200 more (with 100 already invested as the blind) to win a 400-chip pot. The 2:1 immediate pot odds are favorable.

3. Stack Dynamics: My stack (10700) is getting shorter, and while I'm not in push-fold territory yet, I need to play profitable hands. 99 qualifies as one.

4. Flop Equity: If I flop a set (about 11% chance), I'll be in an excellent position. Even if I miss, I can get away from the hand if Minni shows significant strength.

5. Minni's Aggression: Throughout the session, Minni has been quite aggressive from the button. My 99 has reasonable equity against her wide raising range.

This is a straightforward call to see a flop.

Betting with pocket nines on a J-T-2 board where I'm first to act. This is a profitable spot - I'm ahead of most of Minni's range and need to build pots with strong hands given my moderately deep stack. The bet size of 250 is approximately 40% of the pot, which is appropriate for maintaining aggression and getting value while not overcommitting to a potentially dominated hand.

I flopped a set (J♦J♣ with J♥ on the board) — this is almost always best on a dry 2♣‑J♥‑T♥ flop. Claude’s small raise to 250 is either a probe/float or top‑pair/second‑pair attempt; very few hands beat my trips here. I want to build the pot and charge any Jx, Tx, two‑pair combos and overcards that will call. I’ll make a sizeable value 3‑bet to extract and protect (raise to 1500 total). That size is large enough to get value from worse made hands and charge any turn cards, but still leaves room to maneuver on later streets.

Minni's 6x re-raise on a J-T-2 board signals strength. My pocket nines is likely behind or flipping against her probable holdings (overpairs, sets, or broadway hands). With 44 hands remaining and a stack still above 10k, I can afford to be selective and wait for stronger spots. Folding preserves chips for higher-equity situations where I'm more clearly ahead.

With 2♠-10♠ from the button in heads-up play, this is a weak holding that doesn't warrant entering the pot. Despite heads-up ranges being wider, 2-10 offsuit lacks strong playability and flush potential. I'm already at a chip disadvantage (10,200 vs 29,650), and I need to preserve my stack for stronger situations. Folding weak hands and waiting for better opportunities is a fundamental strategy, especially when behind in chips. The cost of calling is minimal (50 more), but the expected value of this hand is negative given Minni's aggressive tendencies and my positional disadvantage.

Heads-up on the button (SB) with pocket nines — a clear open for value and initiative. Pot is 150 and only 50 to call; limp/calling surrenders initiative and gives the BB correct odds. With deep stacks (29.75k vs 10.1k) I want a standard sized raise to build a pot and isolate Claude without overcommitting. A 3xBB-sized raise to 300 extracts value from worse hands (A/Kx, small pairs), folds out some overcards, keeps SPR manageable, and matches table tendencies. If Claude 3-bets I'll re-evaluate based on sizing; versus a call I'll play postflop in position.

Calling is correct in heads-up play with 9-Q from the big blind. The hand has reasonable equity against Minni's opening range, pot odds are favorable (2:1), and I need to defend my big blind to prevent being blinded down given my shorter stack. I'll see the flop and react based on board texture.

I'm holding 9h Qd on a flop of 9s Tc 5c, giving me top pair with a Queen kicker. This is a strong hand in heads-up play on a relatively dry board. Minni has a wide raising range from the button/small blind pre-flop, and top pair is well-positioned against that range. I should bet for value to build the pot while ahead and protect my hand against potential draws (like flush draws with the three clubs on board). A bet of 400 (roughly 65% of the pot) is appropriately sized—substantial enough to get value from weaker hands but not so large that I'm overcommitting with a single-pair hand. This sizing also gives me good flexibility on future streets depending on how Minni responds.

I flopped a set of nines (9c9d on 9s Tc 5c) heads-up — an extremely strong made hand. Claude’s 400 is a relatively small c-bet into a ~1000 pot and represents a wide range (Tx, medium pairs, club draws, air). I want to both extract value from top pairs/medium pairs and charge club draws, so I’ll raise to build the pot and deny cheap turn cards. Raising to 2000 total (i.e. adding 1600 more) should get called by Tx and many pocket pairs/draws while still leaving room to re-evaluate if Claude 3-bets big (in which case his range is polarized to stronger hands and I’ll respond accordingly).

Going all-in with top pair (9h Qd on 9s Tc 5c). My stack has eroded to 9500, making this a critical moment. Minni's 5x raise on the flop could represent a set, strong draw, or aggressive bluff. Top pair with a Queen kicker has good equity against the wide range Minni could hold in heads-up play. By committing all chips now, I avoid being put in a vulnerable spot on future streets where I'd be forced to fold to continued aggression. This gives my hand the best chance to win—either fold equity immediately or by seeing both remaining cards if called. With 150 hands total and only 41 remaining, I need to accumulate chips, and top pair is a reasonable hand to take a stand with.

Flop: 9s Tc 5c. I hold 9c9d — I flopped a set. I raised to 2,000 to charge Tx and medium pairs; Claude’s re-raise to 9,100 looks like an all‑in or near all‑in. Pot is 11,700 and I must put another 7,100 (total committed on the flop would be 9,100). Pot odds: I need ~38% equity to break even (7,100 / (11,700+7,100) ≈ 37.8%). Against Claude’s continuing range (Tx, medium pairs, some bluffs/air), a set is far ahead — the only realistic hands that beat me are pocket tens (very few combos given Tc on the board) or the one remaining nine (quads), both extremely unlikely. Raising further doesn’t extract more value since he’s effectively all‑in and would only create a side pot; folding is throwing away a massive equity favorite. Calling 9,100 total is the clear +EV play to see the turn/river and extract value from worse pairs and Tx hands.

Betting my remaining 800 chips with two pair (9s and 5s with Qd kicker) on the turn. The 5s improved my hand significantly from top pair. With only 800 remaining in a huge pot, I need to commit this with my strong hand rather than check passively and risk being forced to fold. Two pair has excellent equity against Minni's likely calling range, and getting these chips in now maximizes my value.

I hold 9c9d and the board is 9s Tc 5c 5s — I have a full house (nines full of fives). The only hands that realistically beat me are pocket tens (making tens full of fives) or pocket fives (quads) — very few combos. The pot is 19,600 and I only have to call 800 (~3.9% of the final pot), so this is an obvious +EV call to realize my huge equity and collect value from worse pairs/Tx/bluffs. Claude has no remaining chips to reraise, so there’s no reason to fold or try to thinly value-raise. Call the 800.
